back: Traditional delivery method where design is fully completed before construction is bid. Owner holds separate contracts with architect and contractor. Maximum design control for owner; lowest financial risk during design phase.
examTip: Most common delivery method on the ARE. Know the three phases: design â†’ bid â†’ build. Architect has no contract with contractor.

back: Single entity provides both design and construction services under one contract with the owner. Faster delivery, single point of accountability, but owner has less design control and less influence over aesthetics.
examTip: Owner trades control for speed. Architect often works for the contractorâ€”not the ownerâ€”in this model.

back: CM provides a Guaranteed Maximum Price (GMP) and assumes financial risk for construction. Acts as advisor during design and as general contractor during construction, holding trade contracts.
examTip: Key distinction: the 'at risk' CM holds trade contracts and guarantees the GMP. Contractor is selected early, before CDs are complete.

back: CM advises the owner and manages the construction process but does not hold trade contracts or guarantee cost. Owner retains financial risk. CM is a pure consultant with no construction liability.
examTip: No GMP, no trade contracts. Owner is exposed to cost overruns. CMa = advisor only.

back: Contractual model where owner, architect, and contractor share risk, reward, and responsibility through a multi-party agreement. Encourages collaboration from project inception; typically uses BIM and target-cost budgeting.
examTip: IPD = shared risk + shared reward. All key parties sign one contract. Profits and losses are distributed based on project outcomes.

back: Professional liability insurance covering claims arising from the architect's professional acts, errors, or omissions in providing design services. Does not cover intentional acts or guarantees beyond the standard of care.
examTip: E&O = professional liability. Standard of care is the benchmarkâ€”not perfection. The policy covers negligence, not guarantees.

back: Standard agreement defining the architect's scope of basic services (SD, DD, CDs, Bidding, CA), additional services, owner's responsibilities, compensation, and terms of the agreement.
examTip: Know the five phases of basic services. Additional services (site analysis, renderings, LEED) are extra unless listed in Â§4.

back: Fixed-price contract where the contractor agrees to perform the work for a specific total amount. Best used when scope is well-defined. Owner's financial risk is low; contractor assumes cost risk.
examTip: Requires complete CDs before bidding. Owner cannot change scope without a change order. Lowest owner risk once signed.

back: Contractor is reimbursed for actual construction costs plus a fee (fixed or percentage). Used when scope is uncertain. Owner carries cost risk; contractor is motivated to control costs only if fee is fixed.
examTip: Best for fast-track or undefined projects. A GMP cap can be added to limit owner's exposure.

back: The degree of skill and care ordinarily exercised by other architects in the same community under similar circumstances. NOT a guarantee of perfectionâ€”only reasonable competence.
examTip: Architects are held to 'reasonable competence,' not perfection. Exceeding standard of care creates an implied warrantyâ€”avoid it.

back: Business structure where two or more partners share management, profits, and unlimited personal liability. Simple to form; any partner can bind the firm. Each partner is liable for all debts.
examTip: Unlimited personal liability = major risk. In a general partnership, each partner is fully liable for ALL firm debtsâ€”even debts created by other partners.

back: Business entity combining the tax benefits of a partnership with the liability protection of a corporation. Members' personal assets are protected from firm liabilities. Flexible management structure.
examTip: LLC = liability protection + tax pass-through. Members are not personally liable for firm debts. Increasingly popular for small architecture firms.

back: Corporation formed by licensed professionals. Provides some liability protection but shareholders can still be personally liable for their own professional negligence. Required in some states for architecture firms.
examTip: A PC does NOT protect against your own malpracticeâ€”only against others' liabilities and general business debts.

back: Contract provision where one party agrees to defend and hold harmless the other from certain claims or losses. Architects should limit indemnity to their own negligenceâ€”avoid broad indemnification for the contractor's actions.
examTip: Unilateral broad indemnification clauses favor the owner. The ARE presents scenarios where the architect must recognize overreaching indemnity language.

back: Document issued by an insurance company confirming that a party has the required types and amounts of insurance coverage in force. Does not modify the policyâ€”it is evidence only.
examTip: The owner requests COIs from both architect and contractor. It proves coverage exists but does not guarantee claims will be paid.

back: Indirect losses resulting from a breach of contract, such as lost profits, loss of use, or business interruption. AIA contracts include a mutual waiver of consequential damages to limit exposure.
examTip: Mutual waiver = both parties give up the right to sue each other for consequential damages. Protects the architect from catastrophic exposure.

back: Gradual expansion of project scope beyond the original agreement without a corresponding increase in fee or schedule. Major cause of reduced project profitability for architecture firms.
examTip: Track scope creep with additional services authorizations. If the owner adds tasks, document them and bill accordingly. Silence implies acceptance.

back: Percentage of an employee's total work hours that are billable to projects versus overhead activities. Utilization Rate = billable hours Ã· total hours. Healthy firm target: 60â€“65%.
examTip: A low utilization rate means too much time on non-billable workâ€”hurts firm profitability. Time tracking is essential to monitor utilization.

back: Ratio of indirect costs (rent, utilities, admin salaries, marketing) to direct labor costs. Overhead Rate = indirect costs Ã· direct labor. Typical rate is 1.5â€“2.0Ã—.
examTip: A project's fee must cover: direct labor + overhead + profit margin. The multiplier (direct salary Ã— multiplier = billing rate) must exceed 1 + overhead rate to generate profit.

back: The billing level at which revenue exactly covers all direct and indirect costs with no profit. Revenue = Direct Labor Ã— (1 + Overhead Rate). Any billing above this generates profit.
examTip: Break-even = covering all costs without profit. Understanding the multiplier and overhead rate is essential for pricing projects correctly.

back: Document issued by an owner inviting architecture firms to submit qualifications and fee proposals for a project. Defines scope, deliverables, selection criteria, and submission requirements.
examTip: RFP = owner seeking proposals. Distinguish from RFQ (Request for Qualificationsâ€”asks for qualifications only, no fee). Government projects often use a two-step RFQ â†’ RFP process.

back: Reserve funds set aside in a project budget to cover unforeseen conditions or changes. Design contingency covers design changes; construction contingency covers unexpected field conditions.
examTip: Design contingency: 5â€“15% for early phases. Construction contingency: 3â€“10% depending on project complexity. Contingency decreases as design definition increases.

back: Ethical obligations governing AIA members across three tiers: Canons (broad goals), Ethical Standards (conduct expected), and Rules of Conduct (mandatory, enforceable rules).
codeRef: AIA Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct
examTip: Only Rules of Conduct are mandatory and enforceable. Five Canons: (1) General Obligations, (2) Obligations to Public, (3) Obligations to Clients, (4) Obligations to Profession, (5) Obligations to Colleagues.

back: Drawings, specifications, and other documents prepared by the architect are instruments of service and are the architect's property, regardless of whether the project is built. The owner gets a license to use them.
examTip: Architect retains copyright. Owner gets a limited license to use documents for this project only. If the fee is unpaid, the architect can revoke the license.

back: Strategic planning tool identifying a firm's internal Strengths and Weaknesses, and external Opportunities and Threats. Used for business development planning and project pursuit decisions.
examTip: S/W = internal factors (within the firm's control). O/T = external factors (market conditions, competition). Used in strategic planning for firm direction.

back: Project scheduling technique identifying the longest sequence of dependent tasks from start to finish. Any delay on the critical path directly delays project completion. Critical path tasks have zero float.
examTip: Critical path = longest path = 0 float. Float exists only on non-critical paths. Compressing the critical path is called 'crashing.'

back: The amount of time a task can be delayed without affecting project completion. Total float affects the overall schedule; free float affects only the next task. Critical path tasks have zero float.
examTip: Float belongs to the project, not the owner or contractor. If the owner uses contractor float, the contractor may be entitled to a time extension.

back: Written agreement modifying the contract sum, contract time, or both. All three parties must sign: owner, contractor, and architect. Required for all changes to original contract scope after signing.
examTip: All three parties sign. A CCD (Construction Change Directive) allows the owner to direct a change without the contractor's agreementâ€”used when parties can't agree on cost.

back: Written order signed by the owner and architect directing a change in the work when a Change Order cannot be agreed upon. Contractor must proceed; cost and time are determined later.
examTip: CCD = proceed now, negotiate later. Owner and architect sign (not contractor). Used when contractor and owner disagree on cost or time impact.

back: Formal written request from the contractor seeking clarification of contract documents. RFIs clarify intentâ€”they do not change the contract. Architect must respond promptly to avoid contractor claims.
examTip: RFIs clarifyâ€”they don't change. An RFI response that expands scope should trigger a Change Order. Unanswered RFIs can delay the contractor and create cost claims.

back: Shop drawings, product data, and samples provided by the contractor for the architect's review to verify conformance with design intent. Approval does NOT relieve the contractor of responsibility for accuracy.
examTip: Architect reviews for conformance with design intentâ€”not for construction means and methods. Approval is not a guarantee of quality or field accuracy.

back: The stage when the work is sufficiently complete for the owner to occupy and use it for its intended purpose. Triggers the start of the warranty period and the release of retainage.
examTip: Substantial completion â‰  final completion. The architect issues a Certificate of Substantial Completion (G704). Warranty period and retainage release begin at this date.

back: Pre-agreed sum the contractor pays the owner for each day the project is late beyond the contract completion date. Must be a genuine pre-estimate of damages, not a penalty.
examTip: LD must be a reasonable estimate of actual damagesâ€”not punitive. If the delay is owner-caused, the contractor is excused. LDs are often $X per calendar day in the contract.

back: A percentage (typically 5â€“10%) of each progress payment withheld by the owner until substantial completion to ensure the contractor completes all work. Released upon final completion or per contract terms.
examTip: Retainage protects the owner by giving the contractor an incentive to finish. Typically reduced at substantial completion and released at final completion.

back: A breakdown of the contract sum allocated to various portions of work, used as the basis for reviewing contractor's applications for payment. Must be submitted before the first payment application.
examTip: The schedule of values must be approved before payment applications are reviewed. Front-loading (assigning high values to early work) is a red flag for the architect.

back: Contractor's monthly request for payment based on percentage of work completed. The architect reviews and issues a Certificate for Payment if the application is properly submitted and work is as represented.
examTip: Architect has 7 days to act on a payment application. G702 = cover sheet, G703 = continuation sheet (schedule of values breakdown).

back: Written instruction from the architect clarifying or interpreting the contract documents without changing the contract sum or time. If it impacts cost or time, it must become a Change Order.
examTip: ASI = no cost, no time change. If the contractor believes an ASI changes scope, they must notify the architect before proceedingâ€”otherwise they waive the claim.

back: A list of deficient items or incomplete work identified at or near substantial completion that must be corrected before final completion and final payment. Generated by the architect during final inspection.
examTip: A punch list does NOT mean substantial completion is denied. The project can be substantially complete even with punch list items remaining.

back: Report by a geotechnical engineer analyzing soil conditions, bearing capacity, groundwater levels, and foundation recommendations. Owner typically provides this to the architect and contractor.
examTip: The owner is responsible for providing geotechnical data. Concealed conditions (different from the report) can entitle the contractor to a Change Order.

back: Unforeseen physical conditions at the site that differ materially from what was indicated in the contract documents. Entitle the contractor to a Change Order for additional cost and time.
examTip: Two types: (1) Conditions differ from contract docs, (2) Conditions unusual for the work type. Contractor must notify the architect before disturbing the condition.

back: Written communication from the owner to the contractor authorizing work to begin and establishing the contract start date. Contract time begins running from the NTP date.
examTip: Contract time starts with the NTP, not contract signing. Delays in issuing the NTP can give the contractor grounds for a time extension.

back: Delivery approach where construction begins before design is fully complete. Early phases (site, foundations) start while later phases (interiors) are still in design. Compresses schedule but increases risk.
examTip: Fast-track = overlapping design and construction phases. Reduces schedule but increases cost risk (design changes during construction are expensive). Cost-plus contracts are typical.

back: Document issued by the architect to the owner certifying the amount the contractor is owed. The architect acts as an impartial interpreterâ€”not an advocate for either party.
examTip: The architect certifies, not approves. Certification means the work is as represented and the amount is properly calculatedâ€”not that the work is defect-free.

back: Non-binding dispute resolution where a neutral third party helps parties reach a voluntary settlement. Required first step before arbitration or litigation under AIA contracts.
examTip: Mediation is mandatory before arbitration under AIA. Non-binding = no one has to accept the outcome. If mediation fails, parties proceed to binding arbitration or litigation.

back: Binding dispute resolution where a neutral arbitrator hears evidence and issues a final, enforceable award. AIA contracts require arbitration as the default before litigation.
examTip: Arbitration = binding (unlike mediation). Awards are enforceable in court. Typically faster and less expensive than litigation. AIA requires mediation first.

back: Surety bond submitted with a bid guaranteeing that if selected, the bidder will enter into the contract at the bid price. Typically 5â€“10% of the bid amount.
examTip: Bid bond = promise to sign the contract. If the low bidder refuses, the surety pays the difference between their bid and the next lowest bid (up to the bond amount).

back: Surety bond guaranteeing that the contractor will complete the project per the contract terms. If the contractor defaults, the surety either completes the work or pays the owner the bond amount.
examTip: Performance bond = guarantee of project completion. Payment bond guarantees subcontractors/suppliers will be paid. Both are often required on public projects.

back: Document issued by the owner inviting qualified contractors to submit bids. Part of the bidding documents along with Instructions to Bidders, bid forms, and contract documents.
examTip: On public projects, bidding is open and competitive. On private projects, the owner can invite a select list of pre-qualified bidders (selective bidding).

back: Meeting between the owner, architect, and prospective bidders to explain the project and clarify bidding documents. Questions and answers are issued as addenda to all bidders.
examTip: Only addenda are binding clarifications. Verbal answers at a pre-bid conference are NOT binding unless incorporated into an addendum.

back: Written or graphic document issued by the architect before the bid date that modifies, clarifies, or supplements the bidding documents. Must be acknowledged in the bid form to be valid.
examTip: Addenda are issued before bid opening and are binding. All addenda must be acknowledged on the bid form. Contrast with a Bulletin (issued during construction).

back: The number of persons for which a building space is designed, calculated by dividing the floor area by the occupant load factor for the occupancy type. Drives egress design, exit width, and number of exits.
examTip: Assembly (unconcentrated): 15 sf/person. Assembly (concentrated): 7 sf/person. Business: 150 sf/person. Residential: 200 sf/person. Memorize key occupant load factors.

back: Stair egress width = 0.3 inches per occupant. Level egress (corridors, ramps) = 0.2 in/occupant. Minimum stair width: 44 inches (28 in for â‰¤49 occupants).
examTip: Stairs: 0.3 in/occupant. Level egress: 0.2 in/occupant. The 44-inch minimum is independent of occupant load (unless serving fewer than 50 occupantsâ€”then 28 inches).

back: Continuous path connecting accessible elements complying with ADA: min 36-inch clear width, cross-slope max 1:48, firm/stable/slip-resistant surface, running slope max 1:20 (steeper = ramp).
examTip: Accessible route width: 36 in min (60 in at passing spaces). Cross slope: 1:48 max. Running slope above 1:20 requires a ramp with handrails.

back: Minimum floor area for a wheelchair positioned at an element: 30 inches Ã— 48 inches, parallel or forward approach. Must be on an accessible route.
examTip: 30" Ã— 48" = clear floor space at an element. 60" diameter = turning circle. These dimensions appear frequentlyâ€”memorize them.

back: Minimum space for a wheelchair to turn: 60-inch diameter circle OR a T-shaped space (60" Ã— 60" each leg). Required in accessible toilet rooms and at dead-end corridors.
examTip: 60" diameter = turning circle. T-turn space is the alternative. Doors cannot swing into the turning space and reduce it below the minimum.

back: Zoning measure expressing the ratio of a building's total floor area to the lot area. FAR = Total Floor Area Ã· Lot Area. FAR of 2.0 on a 10,000 sf lot = 20,000 sf allowable.
examTip: FAR controls building bulk and density. In NYC, residential FAR ranges from 0.5 in low-density zones to 12.0 in highest-density commercial zones.

back: Permission granted by a zoning board to deviate from dimensional or use requirements due to practical difficulties or unnecessary hardship. Must demonstrate unique site conditions not self-created.
codeRef: Local Zoning Resolution / Board of Zoning Appeals
examTip: Variance â‰  rezoning. Area variance = dimension relief; use variance = different use allowed. Hardship must be unique to the property, not self-inflicted.

back: Net area = usable square footage for the intended function (excludes walls, corridors, mechanical rooms). Gross area = total built area including all structure and service spaces. Net-to-gross ratio: typically 60â€“80%.
examTip: Programming uses net area for space needs. Gross area includes everything. The efficiency ratio (net/gross) shows how much area is 'lost' to structure and services.

back: IBC categorization of buildings by their primary use: Assembly (A), Business (B), Educational (E), Factory (F), High-Hazard (H), Institutional (I), Mercantile (M), Residential (R), Storage (S), Utility (U).
examTip: Occupancy classification drives construction type, sprinkler requirements, egress, and allowable area. Know the Institutional sub-groups (I-1 through I-4) and Residential (R-1 through R-4).

back: IBC classification based on structural materials and fire resistance: Type I (fire-resistive), Type II (non-combustible), Type III (masonry + wood), Type IV (heavy timber), Type V (wood frame). Each has A (protected) and B (unprotected) sub-types.
examTip: Type I-A = highest fire resistance (3-hr structural frame). Type V-B = lowest (no required ratings). Construction type determines allowable height, area, and stories.

back: Three required components: (1) Exit Access â€“ path from occupant to exit, (2) Exit â€“ protected path to exterior, (3) Exit Discharge â€“ path from exit door to public way.
examTip: Exit = the protected portion (enclosed stair, horizontal exit). Exit access = unprotected corridor to the exit. Exit discharge = from exit door to street.

back: Maximum distance an occupant may travel from any point to the nearest exit. Business (B): 200 ft (300 ft sprinklered). Assembly (A): 200 ft (250 ft sprinklered). High-hazard (H-1): 75 ft.
examTip: Sprinklers allow ~50 ft more travel distance in most occupancies. High-hazard occupancies have the shortest limits. Memorize common occupancy travel distances.

back: A corridor with only one means of egress at its end, requiring occupants to retrace their path. Limit: max 20 ft in most occupancies. A dangerous egress condition under emergency.
examTip: Dead-end limit: 20 ft most cases. Exception: 50 ft in Group B with sprinklers. Dead ends require occupants to turn backâ€”avoid or minimize in design.

back: Required exits by occupant load: 1 exit (â‰¤49 occupants), 2 exits (50â€“499), 3 exits (500â€“999), 4 exits (â‰¥1,000). Exceptions exist for occupancy type and sprinklers.
examTip: At 500 occupants, 3 exits are required. At 1,000 occupants, 4 exits. These thresholds are frequently tested. Single-exit buildings are highly restricted.

back: Minimum required distance between a building and the property line (front, rear, or side). Established by local zoning codes to protect light, air, and fire separation between buildings.
examTip: Setbacks define the buildable area. Combined with FAR and height limits, they define the 'zoning envelope'â€”the maximum building volume permitted.

back: Analysis of a proposed project's potential effects on the natural environment. Required for major federal projects under NEPA. Full study = Environmental Impact Statement (EIS); screening = Environmental Assessment (EA).
examTip: NEPA applies to federally funded or permitted projects. An EIS is the full document; an EA is a shorter screening. Finding of No Significant Impact (FONSI) can end the process at the EA stage.

back: Study of the sun's movement across the sky at a specific latitude throughout the year. Key angles: altitude (height above horizon) and azimuth (compass direction). Used for daylighting and shading design.
examTip: In the Northern Hemisphere, south-facing windows get the most sun year-round and are easiest to shade with horizontal overhangs. East/west facades are hardest to shade.

back: Programming tool that diagrams relationships between spaces by indicating which spaces need to be adjacent, which can be near, and which should be separated. Used to develop bubble diagrams.
examTip: An adjacency matrix is a grid where rows/columns are spaces and cells indicate relationship strength. High adjacency = operational efficiency.

back: A document listing all required spaces, their net areas, adjacency requirements, and special conditions. The primary output of the programming phase; it defines the architectural problem before design begins.
examTip: Programming = WHAT. Design = HOW. The space program is the owner-approved baseline. Changes after programming approval should be documented as scope changes.

back: Systematic study of a building's performance after occupancy to assess how well it meets users' needs. Gathers data on occupant satisfaction, energy use, and functional effectiveness.
examTip: POE = feedback loop for design quality. Not a basic service under AIA B101. It closes the loop between programming goals and actual building performance.

back: LEED credits encouraging development on previously developed land, near transit, or in dense urban areas to reduce sprawl, automobile dependency, and environmental impact.
examTip: LEED rewards: brownfield remediation, transit access (1/4 mile of bus, 1/2 mile of rail), and avoiding sensitive habitats (wetlands, prime farmland, floodplains).

back: Schematic diagram showing spatial relationships between program areas as abstract circles. Size represents relative area; lines represent adjacency requirements. Precedes floor plan layouts.
examTip: Bubble diagrams test conceptual organization (zoning), not architectural form. They respond directly to the adjacency matrix. The diagram should reflect programmatic relationships, not final geometry.

back: Multi-story programming diagram showing how program elements are distributed across floors. Used to organize vertical adjacencies and evaluate how mechanical, structure, and program interact floor by floor.
examTip: Heavy floor loads (storage, equipment) go to lower floors. High-ceiling spaces (assembly) may be on grade or upper levels. Stacking affects structural design.

back: Ramp running slope: max 1:12 (8.33%). Width: min 36 inches clear. Max rise per run: 30 inches. Landings required at top, bottom, and each direction change. Edge protection required.
examTip: 1:12 = max slope = 8.33%. A 30-inch rise requires a 30-foot long ramp minimum. Handrails required on both sides for ramps with rise over 6 inches.

back: Required accessible spaces: 1 per 1â€“25 total spaces. Van-accessible space: 8-foot-wide space + 8-foot-wide access aisle (or 11-foot-wide space + 5-foot aisle). Must be on accessible route to entrance.
examTip: 1 in 6 accessible spaces must be van-accessible. Access aisles must be marked 'NO PARKING.' The accessible route from parking to building entrance must comply with slope/width requirements.

back: Permanent, static weight of all structural elements and fixed building components (floors, walls, roofs, ceilings, finishes, fixed mechanical equipment). Does not change over the building's life.
examTip: Dead loads are predictable and permanent. Typical concrete floor: ~150 psf. Steel deck with concrete topping: ~50â€“80 psf. Dead load determines baseline structural member sizing.

back: Variable loads from building occupancy: people, furniture, movable equipment. Office: 50 psf; Residential: 40 psf; Assembly (fixed seats): 60 psf; Assembly (moveable): 100 psf; Storage: 125+ psf.
examTip: Live loads are use-dependent. Assembly and storage occupancies have the highest live loads. The ARE tests whether you know which occupancies have higher vs. lower loads.

back: Vertical structural element resisting lateral forces (wind, seismic) through in-plane shear. Acts as a vertical cantilevered beam. Works with diaphragms to transfer lateral loads to the foundation.
examTip: Shear walls must be continuous from roof to foundation. Their location limits floor plan flexibility. Concrete or CMU cores are common; wood sheathing in light frame construction.

back: Structural system where beams and columns resist lateral forces through rigid connections that allow bending but no rotation at joints. Provides lateral stability without shear wallsâ€”open floor plans possible.
examTip: Moment frames allow open floor plans with no shear walls. More flexible but more expensive than braced frames. Deep beams handle large moments. Used in steel high-rise construction.

back: Measure of thermal resistance of a material or assembly. R = 1/U = thickness Ã· thermal conductivity. Higher R = better insulation. R-values add when layers are stacked in series.
examTip: R-value measures resistance; U-factor measures heat transfer rate (U = 1/R). R-values add in series; U-values do not. ASHRAE 90.1 sets minimum R-values by climate zone.

back: Heat loss through highly conductive materials (metal studs, structural connections) that bypass the insulation layer. Reduces the effective R-value of the assembly significantlyâ€”up to 40â€“50% for metal stud walls.
examTip: Metal studs can reduce wall assembly R-value by 40â€“50% due to thermal bridging. Solutions: continuous exterior insulation, thermal breaks at connections.

back: Material limiting vapor diffusion through building assemblies to prevent condensation within the wall. Class I (foil/polyethylene) most restrictive; Class II (kraft paper) moderate; Class III (latex paint) least restrictive.
examTip: Place vapor retarder on the warm-in-winter side of insulation (interior in cold climates). In hot-humid climates, placement reverses. Wrong placement = interstitial condensation.

back: Moment (rigid) connection transfers bending forces between members. Pin connection transfers only shear and axial forces, allowing rotation at the joint. Moment connections are more expensive.
examTip: Pin = hinged = no moment transfer. Moment = rigid = transfers bending. A beam with two pin supports is statically determinate; a fixed-end beam is indeterminate.

back: IBC classification (A through F) determining seismic design requirements based on site acceleration values and occupancy category. Higher SDC = more stringent structural requirements.
examTip: SDC A = least seismic risk. SDC F = most stringent. Essential facilities (hospitals) in high-hazard areas are bumped up one category. SDC determines which structural systems are permitted.

back: Lateral and uplift forces exerted by wind on building exterior surfaces. Determined by design wind speed, exposure category (terrain), occupancy, and building geometry.
examTip: Wind loads act as pressure on windward face and suction on leeward face. Uplift on flat roofs is significant. Corner regions experience highest pressures (components and cladding).

back: Allowable beam deflection under live load: L/360 (prevents cracking in brittle finishes below). Under total load: L/240. Deflection increases with spanâ´ and decreases with depthÂ³.
examTip: Doubling the span increases deflection 16Ã—. Doubling the depth reduces deflection 8Ã—. L/360 = live load limit. Long spans require deep or pre-cambered members.

back: Concrete system where high-strength steel tendons are tensioned after curing, placing the concrete in compression. Allows longer spans and thinner slabs than conventional reinforced concrete.
examTip: Post-tensioning reduces slab thickness and allows longer spans. Common in parking structures and multi-story residential. Tendon locations must coordinate with MEP penetrations.

back: Three-dimensional structural system of interconnected linear members forming a rigid, lightweight structure. Distributes loads in multiple directions. Used for large-span column-free roofs.
examTip: Space frames work in two directions (unlike standard trusses). Efficient for large column-free spans. Challenge: complex connection details and MEP coordination.

back: Multi-zone HVAC system that varies the volume of conditioned air delivered to each zone based on demand. A single air handling unit (AHU) serves multiple VAV boxes, each controlling one zone.
examTip: VAV is the most common multi-zone system for large office buildings. It's energy-efficient because it reduces air volume (and fan energy) when zones need less cooling.

back: Condition of mind expressing satisfaction with the thermal environment. Influenced by six factors: air temperature, radiant temperature, humidity, air velocity, metabolic rate, and clothing. Standard: ASHRAE 55.
examTip: Six factors = 4 environmental + 2 personal. ASHRAE 55 defines acceptable comfort range. PMV/PPD model predicts satisfaction. Radiant temperature often matters more than air temperature.

back: Quality of air within buildings relative to health and comfort. Governed by ASHRAE 62.1, which sets minimum ventilation rates by occupancy. Key pollutants: COâ‚‚, VOCs, radon, particulates, biological contaminants.
examTip: ASHRAE 62.1 sets minimum ventilation rates. COâ‚‚ above 1,000 ppm signals inadequate fresh air. LEED EQ credits reward improved IAQ beyond minimums.

back: Design strategies using building form, orientation, and materials to collect, store, and distribute solar heat without mechanical systems. Strategies: direct gain (glazing), indirect gain (Trombe wall), isolated gain (sunspace).
examTip: South-facing glazing maximizes winter solar gain. Thermal mass (concrete, masonry) stores heat during day, releases at night. Overhangs shade summer sun while allowing winter sun.

back: Design strategies controlling quantity and quality of stormwater runoff. Techniques: bioswales, rain gardens, green roofs, permeable paving, detention ponds, cisterns.
examTip: LEED rewards reducing runoff quantity and improving quality (removing pollutants). Green roofs retain 50â€“90% of rainfall. Permeable paving reduces runoff but requires maintenance.

back: Design philosophy creating environments usable by all peopleâ€”regardless of age, ability, or sizeâ€”without adaptation or specialized design. Goes beyond ADA minimum requirements.
examTip: 7 Principles: equitable use, flexibility, simple & intuitive, perceptible information, tolerance for error, low physical effort, size & space for approach & use.

back: Ratio of interior illuminance to exterior illuminance under overcast sky conditions, expressed as a percentage. Daylight factor of 2% = adequate for most tasks; 5%+ for high-visual-demand spaces.
examTip: Daylight factor measures how much daylight penetrates the interior. Deeper rooms require higher windows or light shelves. LEED credits reward 75%+ of regularly occupied spaces achieving 300 lux.

back: Sound Transmission Class (STC): single-number rating of a wall, floor, or ceiling assembly's ability to reduce airborne sound. Higher STC = better sound isolation. Hotel room partitions: STC 50.
examTip: STC 50 = hotel/residential standard. Recording studios: STC 60+. Mass (heavy walls) and decoupling (double stud, resilient channels) both improve STC. Flanking paths destroy STC performance.

back: Time in seconds for sound to decay 60 dB after the source stops. Short RT = dry acoustics (speech clarity). Long RT = reverberant (music). Controlled by absorptive surface materials.
examTip: Conference rooms: RT60 < 0.5 sec. Concert halls: RT60 1.5â€“2.5 sec. Adding absorptive material (carpet, acoustic tile, upholstery) reduces RT60. Hard parallel surfaces increase it.

back: Waterproofing resists hydrostatic pressureâ€”required when water can stand against a surface (below-grade walls with water table). Dampproofing only resists soil moisture without hydrostatic pressure.
examTip: Hydrostatic pressure present = waterproofing required. No groundwater pressure = dampproofing may suffice. Habitable below-grade spaces always require waterproofing.

back: Roof covered with vegetation and growing medium over a waterproofing membrane. Extensive: shallow soil (2â€“6 in), sedums. Intensive: deep soil (6+ in), varied plants, higher structural loads (80â€“150+ psf).
examTip: Extensive green roofs add ~10â€“25 psf to roof loads. Both types reduce stormwater runoff and urban heat island effect. Root-resistant membrane is critical.

back: Gap in a structure allowing differential movement between building sections due to thermal expansion, settlement, or seismic forces. Must be sized for expected movement and detailed to be weather-tight.
examTip: Expansion joints separate two independent structural systems. Joint width is determined by thermal movement calculations. Never cross an expansion joint with continuous structure.

back: Non-load-bearing exterior cladding system that hangs from the building structure. Transfers wind and gravity loads to the structure at floor anchors. Types: stick (field assembled) and unitized (factory assembled panels).
examTip: Curtain walls are non-structural. The key design issues are water infiltration, thermal performance, differential movement accommodation, and fire stop at each floor line.

back: Wall cladding strategy using an outer rain screen (cladding) separated from the air/moisture barrier by a drained, back-ventilated cavity. Pressure equalization minimizes water infiltration.
examTip: Rainscreen = outer cladding + drained cavity + inner air barrier. The cavity equalizes air pressure, dramatically reducing water infiltration driving force. Used with brick, metal panel, terracotta.

back: Fire-rated barriers (walls, floors) between different occupancies, construction types, or to protect means of egress. Rating depends on occupancy separation type and construction type.
examTip: Non-separated mixed occupancies: apply the most restrictive requirements throughout. Separated mixed occupancies: fire walls allow each section to stand alone. Know 1-hr vs. 2-hr separation requirements.

back: Standard for the installation of automatic fire sprinkler systems. Defines sprinkler types, spacing, coverage, and pipe sizing. Required by IBC for buildings above certain height, area, or occupancy thresholds.
examTip: NFPA 13 = full sprinkler standard (commercial). NFPA 13R = residential (up to 4 stories). NFPA 13D = one- and two-family. Sprinklers significantly relax egress and construction type requirements.

back: The area of floor or roof that contributes load to a specific structural member. A column at the intersection of two 20-foot bays has a tributary area of 20' Ã— 20' = 400 sf.
examTip: Tributary area determines the load a member must carry. For columns: tributary area Ã— total load (dead + live) = column load. Interior columns carry more load than perimeter columns.

back: Standard organizational system for construction specifications. Division 00: Procurement. Division 01: General Requirements. Division 03: Concrete. Division 05: Metals. Division 07: Thermal & Moisture. Division 08: Openings. Division 26: Electrical.
examTip: Know: 00 (Procurement), 01 (Gen Req), 03 (Concrete), 04 (Masonry), 05 (Metals), 06 (Wood), 07 (Thermal/Moisture), 08 (Openings), 09 (Finishes), 22 (Plumbing), 23 (HVAC), 26 (Electrical).

back: Prescriptive: specifies exact materials, methods, and products (limits contractor choice). Performance: specifies the required result/outcome (contractor chooses means). Most specs combine both.
examTip: Prescriptive = what to use. Performance = what it must do. Performance specs give contractors flexibility but require testing/verification. Proprietary specs name specific products.

back: Addendum: issued before bid opening to modify bidding documents. Bulletin: issued during construction to transmit information (RFI responses, sketch clarifications). Only Change Orders/CCDs modify the contract.
examTip: Addendum = before bids. Bulletin = during construction, informational only. A sketch via bulletin does NOT change the contract priceâ€”that requires a Change Order.

back: Drawings prepared by the contractor or subcontractor showing how a specific product will be fabricated and installed. Architect reviews for conformance with design intentâ€”not for engineering accuracy.
examTip: Architect's review of shop drawings does NOT approve engineering. The contractor remains responsible for dimensions, quantities, and means. Architect checks design conformance only.

back: Complete set of drawings and specifications describing the design in sufficient detail for contractors to bid and build. Legally become part of the contract between owner and contractor.
examTip: CDs are a legal documentâ€”they define what the contractor must build. When conflicts exist between drawings and specs, generally specs govern (unless stated otherwise in the contract).

back: CSI standard format for each spec section: Part 1 â€“ General (scope, submittals, warranties), Part 2 â€“ Products (materials, fabrication standards), Part 3 â€“ Execution (installation, field quality control).
examTip: Every spec section follows 1-2-3. Part 1 = what you submit and warrant. Part 2 = what you supply. Part 3 = how you install it. The ARE tests which part a requirement belongs to.

back: Revised drawings showing actual construction as built, including field changes, routing adjustments, and deviations from original CDs. The contractor's responsibility under AIA A201. Owner's reference for future renovations.
examTip: As-builts are the contractor's responsibilityâ€”the architect is not required to verify them. Critical for future maintenance and often required by owners and code officials.

back: Manufacturer's printed literature, catalog sheets, and technical data submitted by the contractor to document compliance with specification requirements. Part of the submittal process.
examTip: Product data = manufacturer literature. Distinguish from shop drawings (contractor-prepared fabrication drawings) and samples (physical material specimens). All three are submittals.

back: Details referenced from plans or elevations by a key (circle with number/letter) indicating the detail number and sheet where the large-scale drawing can be found. Ensures coordination between drawing scales.
examTip: Keys connect small-scale drawings to large-scale details. Missing or inconsistent keys create coordination errors during bidding or construction.

back: A formal request from the contractor to use a product different from the one specified. Must demonstrate equivalence. Typically allowed only during bidding (with prior approval) unless a specified product is unavailable.
codeRef: AIA A201 Â§3.4.2 / CSI MasterFormat Div 01
examTip: Substitutions require architect approval. 'Equal or better' must be provenâ€”not assumed. Proprietary specs restrict substitutions most severely.

back: Digital representation of a building's physical and functional characteristics. Enables clash detection, quantity takeoff, scheduling (4D BIM), and cost estimation (5D BIM). Level of Development (LOD) 100â€“500.
examTip: BIM is a process, not just software. LOD 300 is required for most coordination. Government projects often mandate BIM (GSA BIM requirements). Clash detection prevents costly field conflicts.

back: Fire resistance ratings for wall, floor, and ceiling assemblies tested and listed by UL (Underwriters Laboratories). Architect specifies a UL Design number; contractor must build the assembly exactly as listed.
codeRef: IBC Â§703 / UL Fire Resistance Directory
examTip: UL listings are prescriptiveâ€”any deviation voids the rating. The architect selects the UL Design; the contractor is responsible for building it correctly. No substitutions without re-testing.

back: First design phase where the architect establishes the project's scale, form, and relationship of spaces. Deliverables: preliminary drawings, outline specs, and a preliminary cost estimate. Owner approves before proceeding to DD.
examTip: SD = concept. Approximately 15â€“35% design development. Cost estimates at SD have Â±20â€“30% accuracy. Major design changes after SD approval can be costly.

back: Second design phase where the architect refines SD, finalizes systems and materials, and coordinates structural, mechanical, and other consultant work. Deliverables: more detailed drawings and updated cost estimate.
examTip: DD = resolution phase. Major systems (structural grid, MEP routing) are fixed. Cost estimate accuracy improves to Â±10â€“15%. Owner approves DD before proceeding to CDs.

back: Tabular drawing or specification listing all doors by number with their size, material, hardware set, frame type, fire rating, and special requirements. Coordinates with door plan symbols and hardware specs.
examTip: Door schedules must be coordinated with hardware specs (hardware sets), fire ratings, and ADA requirements. A fire-rated door requires a rated frame and hardware to maintain the assembly rating.

back: Document issued by the local building department authorizing legal use of a completed building. Issued after final inspections confirm compliance with approved plans and applicable codes.
examTip: No CO = no legal occupancy. The building official issues the COâ€”not the architect. A Temporary CO (TCO) allows partial occupancy before full completion.

back: Inspection of high-risk construction elements (concrete, masonry, high-strength bolting, welding, soils) required by the IBC and performed by an approved Special Inspector.
examTip: Special inspections are required for structural elements where failure risk is high. The owner (not contractor) typically hires the special inspector. A Statement of Special Inspections is submitted with the permit.

back: Periodic site visits by the architect during construction to observe work for general conformance with the contract documents. NOT continuous inspection. Architect reports findings in a Field Report.
examTip: Observation â‰  supervision. The architect is not responsible for the contractor's means, methods, or construction safety. The contractor is solely responsible for construction quality.

back: Written record prepared by the architect after each site visit documenting observations, work in place, weather conditions, issues, and questions. Provides a legal record of the architect's observations.
examTip: Field reports are the architect's legal record. Document what you sawâ€”not what you assumed. Never certify what you didn't observe. Use the AIA G711 form.

back: Work that does not conform to the requirements of the contract documents. The architect can require the contractor to remove and replace nonconforming work, even if it is covered or completed.
examTip: Architect can reject nonconforming work at any time. Covering work before inspection does not excuse it. The architect can require uncovering and remediation at the contractor's expense.

back: Substantial completion: work sufficiently complete for intended use. Triggers warranty period and retainage. Final completion: all punch list items resolved, all submittals received. Triggers final payment.
examTip: Substantial completion triggers: warranty start, retainage reduction, and lien filing deadline. Final completion triggers: final payment and architect's final certificate.

back: Legal claim against a property filed by an unpaid contractor, subcontractor, or supplier. Encumbers the property title until the debt is paid or the lien is bonded off. Must be filed within strict deadlines.
examTip: Lien rights must be perfected within strict deadlines (varies by state). Substantial completion starts the lien clock. Lien waivers are exchanged with payments to clear title.

back: Process of testing, adjusting, and balancing HVAC systems at project completion to verify airflow, temperatures, and system performance meet specifications. Performed by a certified TAB contractor.
examTip: TAB = HVAC systems meet design airflow specs. Commissioning (Cx) goes further: verifying all building systems work together as intended by the Owner's Project Requirements (OPR).

back: Testing of windows, curtain walls, and building envelope to verify resistance to water penetration under specified pressure and water application. ASTM E1105 = field test standard.
examTip: Water infiltration tests apply water and pressure simultaneously. Failed units must be repaired and re-tested. Critical for curtain walls, storefront, and roofing assemblies.

back: Field test measuring the consistency (workability) of fresh concrete by measuring how much a cone of concrete slumps when the mold is removed. Higher slump = more water = lower strength. ASTM C143.
examTip: Slump test is quick but only measures consistency, not strength. Concrete compressive strength is measured by cylinder compression tests at 7 and 28 days (ASTM C39).

back: Compressive strength test on 4"Ã—8" or 6"Ã—12" concrete cylinders taken at the pour. Tested at 7 days and 28 days. 28-day strength is the design strength (f'c). ASTM C39.
examTip: f'c = specified 28-day compressive strength. Typical: 3,000â€“5,000 psi for structural concrete. If 3 consecutive sets average below f'c, the structural engineer must evaluate.

back: Systematic quality assurance process verifying that all building systems are planned, designed, installed, tested, operated, and maintained to meet the owner's project requirements (OPR).
examTip: LEED requires fundamental commissioning for all certified projects. The Commissioning Authority (CxA) is independent from the design team. Enhanced commissioning adds post-occupancy verification.

back: A specified sum included in the contract for items whose exact cost cannot be determined at bid time (e.g., owner-selected tile, lighting fixtures). If actual cost differs, the contract sum is adjusted accordingly.
examTip: Allowances are owner reserves for uncertain items. If spent under = credit to owner. If spent over = additional cost. Allowances should not be used to avoid specifying products.

back: A specified price per unit of work (per cubic yard of excavation, per linear foot of pipe) used when quantities are uncertain at bid time. Quantities are measured in the field; payment is per actual units.
examTip: Unit prices are used when scope quantity is unknown. Common for earthwork, sitework, and underground utilities. Eliminates the need to negotiate Change Orders for quantity variations.

back: Record kept by the contractor documenting daily work activities, workers on site, equipment, weather conditions, and notable events. Used to support claims for time extensions or disruption costs.
examTip: The contractor's daily log is their evidence for schedule claims. The architect keeps a separate site visit log (Field Report). Both are discoverable in dispute resolution.

back: NYC Building Code classifies buildings by construction type: Class I (non-combustible, concrete/steel), Class II (protected combustible), Class III (unprotected combustible). Class I required for high-rises.
examTip: NYC uses its own classification system alongside IBC types. Class I = IBC Type I or II. Know the NYC-specific names for exams that test NYC knowledge.

back: NYC requires Special Inspections for structural work including concrete, masonry, high-strength bolting, and welding. The Special Inspector must be approved by the NYC DOB.
examTip: In NYC, the Special Inspector reports to the ownerâ€”not the contractor. A Statement of Special Inspections must accompany the permit application.

back: Primary legal document governing land use and development in NYC. Establishes use districts, FAR limits, bulk regulations, parking requirements, and special purpose districts.
examTip: ZR is separate from the NYC Building Code. Use the ZR for FAR, setbacks, and use regulations. Use the Building Code for construction standards. Both apply to any NYC project.

back: NYC agency responsible for reviewing building permit applications, conducting inspections, and enforcing the NYC Building Code and Zoning Resolution. Architects file plans with the DOB.
examTip: DOB approves permits, performs inspections, and issues Certificates of Occupancy. Professional Certification (Pro-Cert) allows licensed professionals to self-certify code compliance for DOB.

back: NYC agency designating and regulating individual landmarks, interior landmarks, scenic landmarks, and historic districts. All exterior work on designated buildings requires LPC approval before DOB permits.
examTip: LPC approval comes before DOB filing. A Certificate of Appropriateness (CofA) is the LPC approval document. Even minor changes (window replacement, signage) require LPC review in historic districts.

back: NYC buildings over 75 feet require sprinklers throughout, a Fire Safety Plan, a Fire Safety Director, and stair pressurization. Local Law 5/1973 added retroactive sprinkler requirements for existing high-rises.
codeRef: NYC Building Code Chapter 9 / Local Law 5
examTip: 75 feet = NYC high-rise threshold. All new high-rises must be fully sprinklered. Local Law 5 applies to existing office buildings over 100 ft. NYC requirements are often stricter than IBC.

back: NYC climate law (2019) requiring large buildings (>25,000 sf) to reduce carbon emissions or face annual fines. Emission limits become stricter in 2030. Applies to building operations, not just energy code compliance.
examTip: LL97 is separate from NYCECC. It mandates actual operational carbon reductionsâ€”not just design compliance. Buildings failing to meet limits pay $268 per metric ton of COâ‚‚ over the cap.

back: NYC law requiring accessibility improvements when alterations trigger accessibility upgrades. NYC follows both ADA and NYC Building Code Chapter 11 (which may be stricter). Alterations trigger upgrades to the 'path of travel.'
examTip: In NYC, both ADA (federal) and NYC Building Code accessibility requirements apply. Alterations trigger accessibility upgrades to the path of travel up to 20% of construction cost.

front: AIA B102 â€” Standard Form of Agreement Without Predefined Scope
back: A short-form owner-architect agreement used when project scope is undefined at contract start. Services and compensation are negotiated as the project develops. Commonly used for planning studies, feasibility, and pre-design services.
examTip: B102 is for undefined scope; B101 is for standard full-service projects â€” the exam tests knowing which form fits which situation.

front: AIA B103 â€” Large or Complex Projects Agreement
back: Owner-architect agreement for large, complex projects where the owner has substantial in-house expertise. Includes more detailed scope negotiation, extensive owner-furnished information, and greater owner involvement in project decisions.
examTip: B103 shifts more coordination responsibility to the owner â€” used for hospital systems, universities, and public agencies with dedicated PM staff.

front: AIA A201 â€” General Conditions of the Contract
back: The foundational document defining rights, responsibilities, and relationships among Owner, Contractor, and Architect during construction. Covers submittals, changes, payments, disputes, and termination. Referenced by most AIA construction contracts.
examTip: A201 is incorporated by reference into A101/A102 â€” it governs construction administration. Know which party has which obligation.

back: Standard form for the architect's agreement with sub-consultants (structural, MEP, civil, etc.). Flows down the architect's obligations to the consultant and mirrors the prime contract terms, including confidentiality, insurance, and IP ownership.
examTip: C401 creates a flow-down relationship â€” consultant's liability tracks architect's liability to owner. Know this for coordination and E&O questions.

front: Professional Liability (E&O) Insurance vs. Commercial General Liability (CGL)
back: E&O (Errors & Omissions) covers negligent professional acts â€” design errors and omissions â€” and is claims-made. CGL covers bodily injury and property damage caused by operations â€” occurrence-based. Architects need both; they cover different risk categories.
codeRef: AIA Owner's Guide to Professional Liability Insurance
examTip: E&O is claims-made (policy in force when claim is filed matters); CGL is occurrence-based (policy in force when incident happened matters).

back: Claims-made: covers claims filed during the policy period, regardless of when the incident occurred. Requires 'tail coverage' (extended reporting period) after policy lapses. Occurrence: covers incidents that happen during the policy period, regardless of when the claim is filed. E&O is claims-made; CGL is occurrence.
codeRef: Insurance Institute of America â€” Professional Liability
examTip: If an architect retires and lets E&O lapse without tail coverage, a claim arising from old work is uninsured â€” a common exam scenario.

front: Standard of Care in Architect Negligence
back: The level of skill, care, and judgment ordinarily exercised by competent architects in the same geographic area and time period. NOT a guarantee of perfection. An architect is negligent only if they fall below this standard, not merely because a project has defects.
codeRef: AIA B101 Â§2.2; Common Law Negligence
examTip: Standard of care is a 'reasonable professional' test, not a 'perfect design' test â€” this distinction is frequently tested in PcM questions.

back: Direct costs the architect incurs on behalf of the project, billed to the owner at cost plus an agreed markup. Common examples: reproductions, postage, travel beyond a set radius, renderings, models, permit fees. Listed in AIA B101 Â§11.8.
examTip: Reimbursables are on top of the basic fee â€” the exam tests whether a listed item is a reimbursable expense or part of basic services.

front: Additional Services vs. Basic Services (AIA B101)
back: Basic services are the standard 5 phases (SD, DD, CD, Bidding, CA). Additional services are outside the agreed scope and are separately compensated. Examples of additional: existing conditions survey, BIM modeling beyond standard, post-occupancy evaluation, furniture selection, extended CA.
examTip: If owner requests something outside the original scope, it triggers additional services â€” architect must notify owner in writing per Â§4.2.

front: AIA Code of Ethics â€” Conflict of Interest
back: AIA members must disclose any actual or potential conflict of interest to affected parties. A conflict arises when personal or financial interests may impair professional judgment. Disclosure allows informed consent; undisclosed conflicts violate Canon IV (Obligations to Clients).
codeRef: AIA Code of Ethics, Canon IV, Rule 4.201
examTip: Disclosure alone may be enough; the exam tests whether the architect disclosed and whether the client consented â€” both steps required.

back: The percentage of total employee hours billed to clients (direct labor) vs. total hours worked. Formula: Billable Hours Ã· Total Hours Worked. A healthy utilization rate for an architecture firm is typically 60â€“70%. Below 55% suggests over-staffing or excess overhead.
examTip: Higher utilization = more revenue per dollar of labor cost. The exam may ask you to calculate or interpret utilization rate scenarios.

back: Total indirect (non-billable) costs divided by total direct (billable) labor. Formula: Indirect Costs Ã· Direct Labor Cost. A rate of 1.5 means $1.50 of overhead for every $1.00 of direct labor. Used to set billing rates alongside the direct labor multiplier.
examTip: Overhead rate feeds into the billing rate formula: Billing Rate = Direct Labor Ã— (1 + Overhead Rate + Profit Rate).

back: JV: two or more firms form a new legal entity that contracts with the owner, sharing liability, profits, and losses. Both firms share the project license. Teaming Agreement: firms agree to collaborate on a pursuit but maintain separate legal status â€” one is prime, the other is sub-consultant. JV = shared liability; teaming = prime/sub structure.
codeRef: AIA M101 Memo; AIA Practice Management
examTip: On large public projects, JVs allow firms to meet experience thresholds. Exam tests which structure creates shared licensing and liability.

front: Contingency Fund â€” Owner vs. Contractor
back: Owner's contingency: reserve held by the owner for unknown scope changes, design development increases, or unforeseen site conditions. Typically 5â€“15% of construction cost. Contractor's contingency: reserve within the GMP to cover cost overruns in contractor's scope â€” does not belong to owner if unused in a lump-sum contract.
codeRef: AIA A133 Â§5.1 (GMP); AIA A201 Â§4.3
examTip: Owner's contingency funds owner-directed changes; contractor's contingency covers contractor risk. Know who controls each in GMP contracts.

front: Sole Proprietorship vs. LLC vs. Professional Corporation (PC)
back: Sole proprietorship: unlimited personal liability, simplest structure. LLC: limited liability for members, flexible management, pass-through taxation. PC (or PLLC): required in some states for licensed professionals, provides liability protection but shareholder liability still exists for professional negligence (E&O). Choice affects tax treatment and personal exposure.
codeRef: State licensing statutes; IRS entity classifications
examTip: LLC and PC protect personal assets from business debts â€” but NOT from the architect's own professional negligence (covered by E&O insurance).

front: AIA G704 â€” Certificate of Substantial Completion
back: Document signed by Owner, Contractor, and Architect that establishes the date of Substantial Completion, lists remaining punch list items, assigns responsibility for utilities and insurance, and starts the warranty period. Separate from the final Certificate of Payment (G707).
examTip: G704 starts the 1-year correction period for defective work (AIA A201 Â§12.2.2). Warranty clock begins at Substantial, not Final, Completion.

front: Indemnification Clause â€” Broad vs. Limited Form
back: Broad form: contractor indemnifies architect and owner for ALL losses, including losses caused by the indemnitee's own negligence. Most states prohibit this for construction contracts. Limited (comparative) form: contractor indemnifies only to the extent of contractor's own negligence. AIA A201 uses limited form indemnification.
codeRef: AIA A201-2017 Â§3.18; many state anti-indemnity statutes
examTip: Broad-form indemnification is void in most states â€” the exam tests knowing that AIA uses limited (proportional) indemnification language.

back: Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats â€” a strategic planning framework. Strengths and weaknesses are internal factors (within the firm's control). Opportunities and threats are external factors (market conditions, regulations, competition). Used in business development and practice management planning.
codeRef: AIA Practice Management; Strategic Planning frameworks
examTip: Strengths/Weaknesses = internal (the firm); Opportunities/Threats = external (the market). The exam may present a scenario and ask which category fits.

front: Request for Proposal (RFP) vs. Request for Qualifications (RFQ)
back: RFQ: owner asks firms to submit qualifications (experience, team, approach) to create a short list. No fee is proposed. RFP: issued to short-listed firms; firms submit a detailed proposal including scope, schedule, and fee. Two-step process. Some public owners combine both into a single RFQ/RFP.
codeRef: AIA Procurement Guide; Brooks Act (public sector)
examTip: Under the Brooks Act (federal), QBS (qualifications-based selection) is required â€” fee is negotiated after the most-qualified firm is selected, not proposed upfront.

back: Most states grant reciprocal licensure to architects already licensed in other states, typically requiring proof of current license, NCARB record, and sometimes supplemental exams on local codes. NCARB Certification facilitates reciprocity across all 55 U.S. jurisdictions without retaking the ARE.
codeRef: NCARB Certification; individual state licensing boards
examTip: NCARB Certification is the fastest path to multi-state reciprocity â€” the exam tests the role of NCARB in interstate practice.

front: Total Float vs. Free Float (CPM Scheduling)
back: Total float: the maximum time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project completion date. Free float: the time an activity can be delayed without delaying the early start of any immediately following activity. Free float â‰¤ Total float always.
examTip: Activities on the critical path have zero total float. Free float impacts the next activity; total float impacts the overall project end date.

back: Baseline schedule: the original approved CPM schedule against which actual progress is measured. As-built schedule: documentation of when activities actually occurred, used for delay claims, litigation, and post-project analysis. Deviations between baseline and as-built form the basis for delay claims.
codeRef: AIA A201 Â§3.10; AACE International RP 10S-90
examTip: Baseline vs. as-built comparison is the primary tool for proving delay entitlement â€” the exam may test what document captures actual construction sequence.

front: Excusable vs. Compensable vs. Concurrent Delay
back: Excusable: caused by events beyond contractor's control (acts of God, owner delays) â€” contractor gets time extension but no money. Compensable: caused by owner â€” contractor gets both time and money. Concurrent: both parties responsible simultaneously â€” contractor typically gets time extension but no money for the concurrent period.
codeRef: AIA A201-2017 Â§8.3; Delay claims case law
examTip: When owner and contractor are both responsible for delay at the same time (concurrent), contractor gets time but not additional compensation â€” a common exam trap.

front: Constructive Change vs. Formal Change Order
back: Formal change order: written agreement signed by owner, contractor, and architect adjusting contract sum and/or time per AIA A201 Â§7.2. Constructive change: owner's action or inaction that effectively changes the contractor's work without issuing a formal change order â€” contractor can claim as a breach if uncompensated.
codeRef: AIA A201-2017 Â§7.2 (formal CO); Common law constructive change doctrine
examTip: Constructive changes are unwritten â€” the contractor must document and assert the claim; unreimbursed constructive changes are a leading source of disputes.

back: Pay-when-paid: payment to the subcontractor is due within a reasonable time after the GC is paid by the owner â€” a timing provision only. Pay-if-paid: subcontractor is paid ONLY if the GC receives payment from the owner â€” shifts owner insolvency risk to the sub. Many states prohibit or limit pay-if-paid clauses.
codeRef: AIA A401 Â§11.1; state lien and payment laws
examTip: Pay-if-paid passes the risk of owner non-payment to subcontractors â€” the exam tests whether this is a timing clause or a risk-shifting clause.

front: AIA G702 â€” Application and Certificate for Payment
back: Monthly form submitted by the contractor requesting payment from the owner. Shows scheduled values, work completed this period, materials stored, retainage withheld, and amount due. The architect reviews and, if appropriate, issues the Certificate for Payment within 7 days (AIA A201 Â§9.4).
examTip: The architect's 7-day review window after receiving the application is tested â€” the architect certifies only the amount they determine is properly due, not the contractor's requested amount.

front: AIA G703 â€” Continuation Sheet (Schedule of Values)
back: Attachment to G702 that itemizes the contractor's schedule of values â€” each line item of the contract work with its dollar value and percent complete. Required to support each application for payment and track progress by work category.
examTip: The schedule of values must be submitted at project start and approved by the architect before the first payment application â€” front-loading is a common issue the architect should flag.

back: Mechanic's lien: a claim recorded against the property title by an unpaid contractor, sub, or supplier â€” clouds title and can force a sale to pay the debt. Stop notice: a legal notice to the owner (or lender) to withhold funds from the GC until a sub's claim is resolved. Stop notices are common in states (like CA) that limit lien rights on public property.
codeRef: State lien statutes; AIA A201 Â§9.6
examTip: Public property cannot be liened in most jurisdictions â€” stop notices are the public-project equivalent of a mechanic's lien.

front: Value Engineering (VE) â€” Process and Timing
back: A systematic method to improve the value of goods, products, or services by examining function. In construction, VE identifies cost reductions while maintaining required function. Most effective during SD/DD (before details are fixed). VE during CDs or construction is expensive and creates redesign costs.
codeRef: AIA A201 Â§1.1.1; SAVE International VE Methodology
examTip: VE is most cost-effective before design is fixed â€” the later VE occurs, the more expensive the redesign and the fewer savings realized. This timing is a common exam topic.

front: Bid Alternate â€” Base Bid vs. Additive vs. Deductive Alternate
back: Base bid: the core scope all bidders price. Additive alternate: additional scope that can be added if budget allows (e.g., upgraded finishes, additional area). Deductive alternate: scope that can be removed to meet budget (e.g., delete a building wing). Alternates must be clearly defined in the CDs and bid form.
codeRef: AIA A701 Instructions to Bidders; CSI Project Delivery Manual
examTip: The order in which alternates are accepted must be stated in the bid documents â€” you cannot selectively accept alternates out of order without rebidding.

front: Notice to Proceed (NTP) vs. Contract Execution Date
back: Contract execution date: the date the contract is signed by both parties â€” establishes legal obligations. NTP: written notice from the owner authorizing the contractor to begin work and starting the contract time (calendar or working days). There can be a gap between contract execution and NTP, during which the contractor is not yet mobilizing.
examTip: Contract time begins on the date stated in the NTP or on the date of commencement stated in the contract â€” not necessarily on the signature date.

front: AIA G707 â€” Consent of Surety to Final Payment
back: Document obtained from the contractor's surety (bonding company) consenting to the release of final payment. Required by AIA A201 Â§9.10.3 before the architect certifies final payment. Protects the owner by ensuring the surety agrees that all obligations have been met.
examTip: Final payment cannot be certified without the G707 consent of surety â€” this is a checkpoint the exam tests in the CA phase sequence.

front: Earned Value Management (EVM) â€” Key Metrics
back: EVM measures project performance using: PV (Planned Value = budgeted cost of work scheduled), EV (Earned Value = budgeted cost of work performed), AC (Actual Cost = actual cost of work performed). Schedule Variance = EV âˆ’ PV; Cost Variance = EV âˆ’ AC. Negative variance = behind schedule or over budget.
examTip: EV âˆ’ AC = Cost Variance (negative = over budget). EV âˆ’ PV = Schedule Variance (negative = behind schedule). The exam may present numbers and ask what they indicate.

back: Services provided by the CM or GC before the construction documents are complete: constructability review, cost estimating, scheduling, subcontractor pre-qualification, phasing analysis, and long-lead procurement. Paid as a separate fee; helps refine the GMP before construction starts.
examTip: Pre-construction services are unique to CMc/GMP delivery â€” they allow early contractor input before design is fixed, reducing change orders during construction.

back: A list of all submittals (shop drawings, product data, samples) required by the contract documents, with planned submission dates and review turnaround times. Submitted by the contractor and reviewed/approved by the architect. Required by AIA A201 Â§3.10.2 as part of the construction schedule.
examTip: The submittal schedule establishes the review pipeline â€” if the architect fails to review submittals in time, it may constitute an owner-caused delay.

back: An alternative to subcontractor performance and payment bonds. The GC purchases SDI to cover costs if a subcontractor defaults â€” without requiring each sub to obtain a bond. Provides faster response than surety bonds. Owner typically requires evidence of SDI or bonds in the prime contract.
codeRef: AIA A201 Â§11.1; Zurich SDI product documentation
examTip: SDI is GC-purchased and owner-benefited â€” unlike traditional bonds where the sub provides the surety. Exam may test who purchases and who benefits.

back: A project delivery approach where design and construction are overlapped â€” construction begins on completed portions while design of later portions continues. Reduces overall project duration but increases the risk of design changes affecting already-constructed work and can increase change order costs.
codeRef: AIA A201 Â§3.10.1; AIA Project Delivery Guides
examTip: Fast-track compresses schedule but increases coordination risk and potential rework costs â€” the exam tests the trade-off between time savings and cost risk.

back: A type of surety bond submitted with a bid guaranteeing that the bidder will enter into the contract at the bid price if selected. If the low bidder refuses to sign, the bid bond compensates the owner for the difference between the defaulting bid and the next accepted bid (typically up to 5â€“10% of bid amount).
codeRef: AIA A310 Bid Bond; AIA A701 Instructions to Bidders
examTip: Bid bond protects the owner from a low bidder backing out after award â€” the bond amount (not the full bid) is the maximum exposure to the owner.

back: Performance bond: guarantees the contractor will complete the contract per its terms. If contractor defaults, surety completes the work or pays damages up to the bond amount (typically 100% of contract value). Payment bond (Miller Act equivalent): guarantees the contractor will pay all subs, suppliers, and laborers. Required on federal public projects over $150,000.
codeRef: AIA A312; Miller Act (40 U.S.C. Â§3131)
examTip: Performance bond protects the owner from contractor default; payment bond protects subs and suppliers. Both are typically required together on public projects.

back: An estimate of the remaining cost to finish a project, used in project accounting to forecast final project cost. Final Cost = Actual Cost to Date + Cost to Complete. When compared to the original contract value, reveals cost overruns or savings. Updated regularly (monthly) during construction.
codeRef: AIA G702 cost tracking; PSMJ Project Management
examTip: Cost-to-complete projections are the contractor's tool for financial control â€” the exam may test what a high CTC relative to remaining contract balance indicates.

front: BOMA Gross Area vs. Rentable Area vs. Usable Area
back: Gross area: total built area measured to exterior face of exterior walls. Rentable area: tenant's share of floor plus a pro-rata share of common areas (lobbies, corridors, restrooms) â€” the basis for lease rates. Usable area: area exclusively occupied by the tenant. Rentable = Usable Ã— Load Factor.
codeRef: BOMA International Standard Method of Measurement (2017)
examTip: Load factor (R/U ratio) is the multiplier from usable to rentable â€” typically 1.10â€“1.20. The exam tests the hierarchy and what each measurement includes.

back: The portion of the exit access that occupants must traverse before they have a choice of two separate exits. IBC limits common path of travel: 75 ft. for most occupancies (Group A, B, E, F, H, I, M, R, S, U) and 100 ft. for sprinklered B and S occupancies; 20 ft. for Group H occupancies.
examTip: Common path of travel is measured before exit options diverge. Exceeding the limit requires adding an additional exit or reconfiguring the path â€” frequently tested in PA section.

back: A mezzanine is an intermediate level between a floor and ceiling. Per IBC Â§505.2, total mezzanine area within a room cannot exceed 1/3 of the area of the room below. If it does, it is treated as an additional story. Mezzanines are typically open to the room below and may share the means of egress.
examTip: 1/3 rule is the key number â€” exceed it and the mezzanine becomes a floor, triggering additional exit requirements and construction type compliance.

front: Area Increase for Automatic Sprinkler System (IBC)
back: IBC Table 506.2 allows increases to the allowable building area when an NFPA 13 sprinkler system is installed: 200% increase per sprinkler increment for multi-story buildings. For single-story buildings, the area increase can be unlimited for many occupancies when fully sprinklered.
examTip: Sprinkler increases are multiplicative, not additive â€” a sprinklered single-story Group B building can often be unlimited area. Know the formula: At = [Aa + (Aa Ã— If)].

front: Construction Type I-A vs. V-B â€” Fire Resistance
back: Type I-A: highest fire resistance â€” structural frame must be 3-hr rated, bearing walls 3-hr, floor/ceiling assemblies 2-hr. Type V-B: lowest â€” no fire resistance required for any element. Used for small buildings where proximity to property line, occupancy, and height/area limits allow unprotected combustible construction.
examTip: Type I-A is used for high-rises and large hospitals; V-B for small wood-frame buildings. Know that construction type and sprinklers together determine allowable height and area.

back: A building with an occupied floor located more than 75 feet above the lowest level of fire department vehicle access. High-rises trigger special requirements: NFPA 13 sprinklers throughout, emergency voice/alarm system, fire command center, pressurized stairways, and standby power for life safety systems.
examTip: 75 ft. is the threshold â€” measured to the highest occupied floor, not the building's total height. The exam uses this to trigger or exempt additional requirements.

back: When different occupancies are in the same building (separated occupancy method), fire barriers are required between them. The rating depends on the occupancy pair â€” e.g., A-2 vs. B requires a 2-hr separation; H vs. most others requires 2â€“4 hrs. Sprinklers may reduce the rating by 1 hour in some cases.
examTip: The exam gives two occupancy groups and asks the required separation rating. Always check the table both ways â€” the higher rating governs.

back: ADA Standards Table 208.2: 1 accessible space per 25 total spaces for the first 100 spaces; then decreasing ratios for larger lots. Van-accessible spaces: 1 per every 6 accessible spaces (or at least 1 van space). Must be on the shortest accessible route to the building entrance.
examTip: Know the 1-in-25 ratio for the first 100 spaces and that van-accessible means 1 in 6 accessible spaces â€” both ratios are testable.

back: A building that produces as much energy as it consumes on an annual basis, typically through on-site renewable energy (solar PV, wind) combined with highly efficient envelope and mechanical systems. NZEB can be net-zero source energy, net-zero site energy, or net-zero energy cost depending on the definition used.
codeRef: NREL Net Zero Energy Building definitions; ASHRAE 189.1
examTip: Net zero site energy = equal energy in/out at the building meter. Net zero source energy accounts for grid transmission losses â€” source is harder to achieve. Know the distinction.
