back: LEED v4 BD+C credit categories: Integrative Process (IP), Location & Transportation (LT), Sustainable Sites (SS), Water Efficiency (WE), Energy & Atmosphere (EA), Materials & Resources (MR), Indoor Environmental Quality (IEQ), Innovation (IN), Regional Priority (RP). Certification levels: Certified (40â€“49 pts), Silver (50â€“59), Gold (60â€“79), Platinum (80+).
examTip: Energy & Atmosphere has the most available points in LEED v4 BD+C â€” the exam tests the category structure and minimum program requirements (MPRs).

front: Adaptive Reuse â€” Zoning and Code Considerations
back: Conversion of an existing building to a new occupancy class. Triggers: change-of-occupancy review under IBC Chapter 10 and local zoning, accessibility upgrades (ADA path of travel), energy code compliance to the extent technically feasible, and zoning conformity (the new use must be permitted in the zone or a variance obtained).
codeRef: IBC Chapter 34; IBC Â§3408; ADA Â§202 Path of Travel
examTip: Adaptive reuse activates 'path of travel' ADA requirements â€” up to 20% of the alteration cost must be spent on accessibility upgrades to the path of travel to the altered area.

front: Means of Egress â€” Three Required Components (IBC)
back: 1. Exit Access: the portion of means of egress from the occupied space to the exit â€” includes corridors, aisles, intervening rooms. 2. Exit: the protected path (enclosed stairway, exterior door, horizontal exit). 3. Exit Discharge: from the exit termination to the public way â€” must be at grade and lead to a public way.
examTip: The three components are sequential and each has its own travel distance and width requirements. Confusing exit access with exit is a common error on the exam.

back: An ultra-low energy building performance standard requiring: airtight envelope (â‰¤0.6 ACH50), triple-pane windows, continuous insulation, thermal bridge-free detailing, and heat recovery ventilation (HRV/ERV). PHIUS targets are climate-specific and certified by Passive House Institute US. Reduces heating/cooling loads by up to 90%.
examTip: Passive House differs from LEED by focusing on measured energy performance metrics (airtightness, heating/cooling loads) rather than credit-point system.

back: FAR = Total Gross Floor Area Ã· Total Lot Area. A FAR of 2.0 on a 10,000 SF lot allows 20,000 SF of building floor area. FAR does not limit height directly â€” it limits total floor area. Underground parking and mechanical penthouses may be exempt depending on local zoning code.
codeRef: NYC Zoning Resolution Â§12-10; local zoning ordinances
examTip: FAR controls density, not height. You can build a tall, slender building or a low, wide one â€” as long as total floor area does not exceed FAR Ã— lot area.

front: Topographic Survey â€” Components and Purpose
back: A survey that maps the existing site conditions including: spot elevations, contour lines, existing structures, utility locations, trees (with species and caliper), easements, property lines, and benchmarks. Required for site analysis, drainage design, and submission to most building departments.
codeRef: ALTA/NSPS Land Title Survey standards; local survey requirements
examTip: The topographic survey is an owner-furnished item (AIA B101 Â§5.4) â€” the architect uses it but does not prepare it. Know who is responsible for providing it.

front: Zoning Variance vs. Special Use Permit
back: Variance: relief from specific dimensional or use requirements of the zoning ordinance (e.g., reduced setback) granted by a Zoning Board of Appeals. Special Use Permit: allows a use that is conditionally permitted in the zone but requires case-by-case review (e.g., a school in a residential zone). Variance = exception to the rule; special use = conditional approval within the rule.
codeRef: Local zoning ordinances; Standard Zoning Enabling Act
examTip: A variance changes the standard for one property; a special use permit applies a standard use with conditions â€” the exam tests which process is appropriate for a given scenario.

front: Sun Path and Solar Analysis in Site Design
back: Sun path diagrams show the sun's altitude and azimuth throughout the year for a specific latitude. Used to determine: optimum building orientation (long axis east-west for north-south solar gain), shading from adjacent buildings or topography, and overhangs for summer shading while allowing winter sun.
codeRef: Sun angle calculator tools; ASHRAE 90.1 orientation guidance
examTip: South-facing facades in the Northern Hemisphere receive the most winter sun and are easiest to shade in summer with horizontal overhangs â€” a fundamental passive solar principle.

back: IBC Â§1020.4 limits dead-end corridors to 20 ft. in most occupancies (Group B sprinklered: 50 ft.). A dead-end corridor is one from which there is only one direction of travel to an exit â€” it increases travel distance in an emergency.
examTip: 20 ft. is the basic limit â€” memorize it. Exceeding it requires either adding a second exit path or reconfiguring the corridor. Sprinklers may allow longer dead ends in some occupancies.

back: A systematic evaluation of a building's performance after occupants have been using it for a period (typically 1â€“2 years). Assesses whether the design meets user needs, identifies performance gaps, and informs future projects. Can include surveys, energy data review, and walkthroughs. Listed as an Additional Service under AIA B101.
codeRef: AIA B101 Â§4.2.4; ASHRAE Building EQ
examTip: POE is an additional service â€” not included in basic services. It closes the feedback loop between design intent and built reality.

back: Stacking diagram: a multi-story building diagram showing which departments or functions occupy which floors â€” used early in programming to allocate vertical adjacencies. Blocking diagram: shows horizontal space allocation on a single floor plan, subdividing the floor plate into functional zones. Together they evolve into schematic layouts.
examTip: Stacking = vertical floor-by-floor allocation; blocking = horizontal zone allocation on a floor. Both precede bubble diagrams and schematic floor plans.

front: Two-Way Slab vs. One-Way Slab â€” Span Ratio Rule
back: A slab is considered two-way (spanning in both directions) when the long-to-short span ratio is â‰¤ 2:1. When the ratio exceeds 2:1, the slab behaves as one-way and reinforcement runs primarily in the short direction. Two-way slabs are more material-efficient for large, square bays.
examTip: L/S â‰¤ 2.0 â†’ two-way slab. L/S > 2.0 â†’ one-way slab. This is the primary design decision in concrete flat-plate and flat-slab structures.

back: A seismic isolation system that decouples the building superstructure from ground motion by placing flexible isolators (lead-rubber bearings or friction pendulum systems) at the foundation level. Reduces building accelerations and story drift. Used for hospitals, emergency facilities, and high-value structures in high-seismic zones.
examTip: Base isolation protects building contents and allows the building to function post-earthquake â€” it shifts the fundamental period away from ground motion frequencies.

front: Hollow Core Precast Plank vs. Cast-in-Place Slab
back: Hollow core: prestressed precast concrete planks with longitudinal voids, typically 6â€“12 in. deep, spanning 20â€“40 ft. Factory-produced, fast to install, requires a topping slab for diaphragm action. CIP slab: cast on formwork in place â€” more flexible for irregular geometry, longer lead time. Hollow core is cost-effective for repetitive residential and parking structures.
examTip: Hollow core requires a poured concrete topping (typically 2 in.) to act as a structural diaphragm â€” without it, lateral loads cannot be transferred to shear walls.

front: Cavity Wall (CMU) vs. Single-Wythe CMU Wall
back: Single-wythe: one layer of CMU â€” simpler, lower cost, limited thermal performance. Cavity wall: inner and outer wythes separated by an air space (typically 2â€“4 in.) â€” allows insulation in the cavity, moisture management with weep holes, and superior thermal performance. Cavity walls use metal ties to connect wythes.
examTip: Cavity walls drain moisture through flashing and weep holes â€” the air gap is the key moisture management feature. The exam tests the functional difference between the two systems.

front: Retaining Wall Types â€” Gravity vs. Cantilever vs. Counterfort
back: Gravity: mass resists overturning â€” thick unreinforced concrete or masonry. Cantilever: reinforced concrete L-shape or T-shape â€” wall stem acts as a cantilever from the base footing. Counterfort: cantilever with triangular reinforced ribs (counterforts) on the retained-earth side, used for tall walls (>25 ft.) to reduce stem bending.
examTip: Cantilever retaining walls are most common for walls 8â€“25 ft. The base footing extends into the retained earth â€” soil weight on the heel helps resist overturning.

front: VAV (Variable Air Volume) HVAC System â€” Operation
back: VAV delivers conditioned air at a constant supply temperature but varies the volume of air delivered to each zone based on load. Each zone has a VAV box with a damper. Energy-efficient at part-load conditions. Requires a large central air handling unit and extensive ductwork. Common in commercial office buildings.
examTip: VAV saves energy by reducing fan speed (via VFD) as zone loads decrease â€” compared to constant volume systems that reheat excess conditioned air.

back: ASHRAE 90.1 sets maximum LPD (watts per square foot) for interior spaces. Example values (90.1-2019): Office â€” 0.79 W/SF; Corridor â€” 0.41 W/SF; Retail â€” 1.05 W/SF; Classroom â€” 0.71 W/SF. LPD compliance is a prerequisite for LEED EA credit and is the basis for energy code lighting compliance.
examTip: LPD limits must be met or exceeded using the space-by-space or building-area method. The exam may ask which space has a higher LPD allowance.

front: Daylight Autonomy (DA) and Useful Daylight Illuminance (UDI)
back: Daylight Autonomy: percentage of occupied hours per year that a space meets its target illuminance level from daylight alone. Useful Daylight Illuminance (UDI): percentage of hours that daylight levels fall within a 'useful' range (typically 100â€“3,000 lux) â€” avoiding both underlit and glare conditions. Both are annual climate-based metrics (CBDM).
codeRef: IESNA Lighting Handbook; LEED v4 IEQ Credit Daylight
examTip: DA measures daylight sufficiency; UDI measures daylight usefulness including glare avoidance. Both require hourly climate data simulations â€” not simple rules of thumb.

front: STC Rating vs. NRC vs. IIC â€” Acoustic Metrics
back: STC (Sound Transmission Class): rates a partition's ability to block airborne sound transmission â€” higher is better (STC 50+ for private offices). NRC (Noise Reduction Coefficient): rates a surface's ability to absorb sound â€” 0 (no absorption) to 1.0 (full absorption). IIC (Impact Insulation Class): rates a floor-ceiling assembly's resistance to impact sound (footsteps) â€” higher is better.
codeRef: ASTM E90 (STC); ASTM C423 (NRC); ASTM E492 (IIC)
examTip: STC = partition blocking; NRC = surface absorption; IIC = impact/footfall through floors. Know which metric applies to which acoustic problem.

front: Thermal Mass â€” Effect on Peak Loads
back: High thermal mass materials (concrete, masonry, water) absorb heat during the day and release it at night, shifting peak cooling loads and reducing temperature swings. Most effective in climates with high diurnal temperature variation (hot days, cool nights). In consistently hot or cold climates, insulation is more important than mass.
codeRef: ASHRAE Fundamentals Handbook Chapter 18; IBC Â§1403
examTip: Thermal mass works best with large day-night temperature swings â€” it delays, not eliminates, the cooling load. Night ventilation discharges stored heat effectively.

front: Curtain Wall System â€” Stick vs. Unitized
back: Stick: components (mullions, transoms, glass) shipped separately and assembled piece-by-piece on site. Lower fabrication cost, slower installation, more field joints. Unitized: factory-assembled panels one story tall, shipped complete to the site and hung from floor to floor. Faster installation, better quality control, higher unit cost. Unitized preferred for tall buildings.
examTip: Unitized curtain wall is faster and more consistent but costlier per unit. Stick is better for small or complex geometries where factory assembly is impractical.

back: A cladding system with a ventilated air gap between the outer cladding and the weather-resistive barrier (WRB) behind it. The outer cladding is not waterproof â€” it deflects bulk water. The air gap allows drainage and drying. Pressure equalization in the cavity minimizes water infiltration. Requires weep holes and open joints at base.
codeRef: AAMA 508; Building Science Corp. rainscreen research
examTip: Rainscreen works by pressure equalization and drainage â€” not by creating a waterproof outer skin. The WRB behind the gap is the true waterproofing layer.

front: Expansion Joint vs. Control Joint vs. Isolation Joint
back: Expansion joint: a complete break through the building (structure, cladding, finishes) to allow differential thermal movement. Control joint: a scored or raked groove in concrete or masonry that controls where cracking occurs â€” does not go full depth. Isolation joint: separates a slab from a column or wall so they move independently.
examTip: Expansion joint goes all the way through; control joint guides cracking without being a full break; isolation joint allows independent movement at element interfaces.

front: Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) â€” Four Stages
back: LCA evaluates environmental impact across a product's full life: A1-A3 Product (raw material extraction, transport, manufacturing), A4-A5 Construction (transport to site, installation), B1-B7 Use (maintenance, repair, replacement, energy use), C1-C4 End of Life (demolition, transport, waste processing). Embodied carbon = A1-A5.
examTip: A1-A3 (manufacturing) represents most embodied carbon for typical building materials. Operational carbon (B6 energy use) has historically dominated but embodied is growing in importance.

front: BIM Level of Development (LOD) â€” 100 to 500
back: LOD 100: conceptual representation â€” elements not yet geometry (symbolic). LOD 200: approximate geometry, size, shape, location. LOD 300: specific geometry, accurate size and location â€” suitable for coordination. LOD 350: adds interfaces with other systems. LOD 400: fabrication-level detail. LOD 500: as-built â€” field-verified geometry and data.
codeRef: AIA E203 BIM Protocol; BIMForum LOD Specification
examTip: LOD 300 is the typical milestone for CD coordination (clash detection). LOD 500 is the as-built record model. Know that LOD increases with design development stage.

back: Exchanges heat with the ground (constant ~55Â°F year-round) via a closed-loop field of buried pipes. Highly efficient: COP of 3â€“5 (vs. 2.5 for air-source heat pumps). Can heat in winter and cool in summer using the same equipment. Vertical bore holes (150â€“300 ft. deep) used on constrained urban sites; horizontal loops on larger sites.
codeRef: ASHRAE 90.1; ASHRAE HVAC Applications Handbook Chapter 34
examTip: Ground-source beats air-source in efficiency because ground temperature is stable year-round â€” the exam tests the basic principle and why it outperforms conventional systems.

front: Green Roof â€” Extensive vs. Intensive
back: Extensive: shallow growing medium (2â€“6 in.), drought-tolerant sedums, lightweight (15â€“50 psf wet), low maintenance, not accessible for planting/occupation. Intensive: deep growing medium (8â€“24+ in.), can support trees and lawns, heavy (100â€“150+ psf wet), requires irrigation and maintenance, accessible as a usable amenity.
codeRef: ASTM E2397; FLL Green Roof Guidelines
examTip: Extensive green roofs are the typical default for stormwater management â€” they require minimal added structural capacity. Intensive requires significant structural upgrade.

front: Moment Frame vs. Shear Wall â€” Lateral System Comparison
back: Moment frame: rigid connections between beams and columns resist lateral loads through bending â€” allows open floor plans with no walls required in the structural bay. Shear wall: a solid wall (concrete, masonry, or plywood) that acts as a deep beam, resisting lateral loads through shear â€” limits planning flexibility but is stiffer and less expensive for low-rise.
codeRef: ASCE 7-22 Chapter 12; AISC Seismic Design Manual
examTip: Moment frames are ideal for open plans (no walls needed); shear walls add stiffness and are cheaper but restrict planning. High-rise buildings often combine both.

front: Waterproofing vs. Dampproofing â€” When to Use Each
back: Dampproofing: a coating that resists moisture vapor diffusion â€” used where hydrostatic pressure is absent (above-grade or shallow below-grade walls in well-draining soil). Waterproofing: a membrane system designed to resist hydrostatic water pressure â€” required for below-grade walls where groundwater table may rise, elevator pits, and planters.
codeRef: IBC Â§1805; ASTM D449; ASTM D6135
examTip: If hydrostatic pressure can occur (water table within 8 ft. of foundation), waterproofing is required â€” dampproofing is inadequate. This distinction is frequently tested.

front: Tributary Area â€” Structural Load Distribution
back: The floor or roof area that contributes load to a specific structural member (beam, column, wall). For a column, the tributary area is the surrounding bay area divided among adjacent columns. Tributary area determines the total load a member must carry: Load on Member = Tributary Area Ã— Load per Unit Area (psf).
codeRef: ASCE 7-22 Â§4.3; ACI 318; AISC Steel Construction Manual
examTip: Larger tributary area = more load on the member. Double the tributary area = double the load. The exam tests tributary area calculation for simple rectangular bays.

back: Division 00: Procurement and Contracting Requirements â€” includes invitation to bid, instructions to bidders, bid forms, bonds, and the contract itself. Division 01: General Requirements â€” includes summary of work, allowances, unit prices, submittal procedures, temporary facilities, and closeout requirements. Division 00 is pre-contract; Division 01 governs construction execution.
examTip: Division 00 = before contract (procurement); Division 01 = contract administration requirements that apply to all work. This distinction is tested in CD and bidding phase questions.

back: Specifies requirements for the contractor's submittal process: what must be submitted, how many copies, review turnaround time, and the workflow. Sets out responsibilities for shop drawings, product data, and samples. The architect's review schedule is based on this section.
examTip: Section 01 33 00 establishes the submittals baseline â€” if a contractor skips a required submittal, this section is the contractual basis for requiring compliance.

back: Specifies the contractor's obligation to maintain as-built record documents (marked-up drawings showing field changes), which are turned over at project closeout. Architect reviews record documents before certifying final payment. These differ from as-built drawings prepared by the architect, which are an Additional Service.
examTip: Record documents are the contractor's responsibility under AIA A201 â€” the architect reviews but does not prepare them. An architect-prepared as-built drawing is an additional service.

front: Permit Set vs. Bid Set vs. Construction Set
back: Permit set: submitted to the building department for plan check â€” may not be 100% complete. Bid set (IFB): complete enough for contractors to price all work â€” issued for competitive bidding. Construction set (for construction): the final, permitted, and coordinated set used in the field â€” typically the bid set plus addenda and permit corrections.
codeRef: AIA B101 Project Phases; AIA A701
examTip: Permit and bid sets are often issued simultaneously but may diverge â€” the construction set must incorporate all permit corrections and addenda before field use.

front: Proprietary Spec vs. Descriptive Spec vs. Reference Standard Spec
back: Proprietary: names a specific manufacturer and model (e.g., 'Andersen Series 400 window') â€” restrictive but guarantees the intended product. Descriptive: lists physical and performance characteristics without naming a brand. Reference standard: incorporates a published standard (e.g., 'ASTM A615 Grade 60 rebar'). Most specs use a combination.
codeRef: CSI Project Delivery Practice Guide; AIA MasterSpec
examTip: Proprietary specs with 'no substitutions' are restrictive and may limit competition. Public projects often require open specs with 'or equal' language to ensure competitive bidding.

back: Names a specific product as the design basis, with performance criteria, while allowing approved equal substitutions that meet all listed requirements. Balances design intent specificity with competitive procurement. The named product establishes the standard; substitutions must demonstrate equivalence in all respects.
codeRef: CSI Project Delivery Practice Guide; AIA Specification Practice
examTip: Basis-of-design gives the architect control over quality while avoiding sole-source restrictions â€” commonly used on public projects that require competitive bidding.

back: A fixed dollar amount included in the contract sum for a specific item whose scope or cost cannot be fully determined at the time of bidding (e.g., tile allowance, owner-furnished equipment). If actual cost differs, a change order adjusts the contract sum. Allowances are defined in Division 01 and itemized in the bid form.
examTip: Allowances are placeholders for undefined cost â€” the contractor's overhead and profit are typically not included in the allowance amount but are added when the final cost is known.

back: A pre-agreed price per unit of work (e.g., $50/LF for concrete curb, $25/CY for excavation) used when quantities cannot be precisely determined before construction. The contract sum adjusts based on actual quantities measured in the field. Protects both owner and contractor from quantity uncertainty.
examTip: Unit prices are used for earthwork, utility lines, and other quantity-variable items. The exam tests who benefits when actual quantity is less than or greater than estimated.

front: Testing and Inspection â€” Who Pays (AIA A201)
back: The owner pays for testing and inspection services, including special inspections, unless the tests reveal nonconforming work â€” in that case, the contractor pays for retesting. AIA A201 Â§13.5 assigns owner responsibility for initial testing; contractor pays for any retesting required because work failed.
examTip: Owner pays for testing; contractor pays for retesting if work fails. This allocation is frequently tested â€” an owner cannot shift routine inspection costs to the contractor.

front: CSI Three-Part Specification Format â€” Part 1, 2, 3
back: Part 1 General: administrative and procedural requirements â€” submittals, delivery, storage, project conditions, warranty. Part 2 Products: materials, manufactured units, fabrication, and finishes. Part 3 Execution: installation, erection, application, field quality control, and closeout. All technical sections follow this structure.
examTip: Part 1 = administrative; Part 2 = what you buy; Part 3 = how you install it. The exam may give a requirement and ask which Part it belongs in.

front: Drawing Coordination â€” Structural vs. Architectural Conflict
back: When structural drawings conflict with architectural drawings, the structural drawings generally govern for members and sizes; architectural govern for finishes and room layouts. The AIA A201 Â§1.2.1 hierarchy: Contract Documents must be read together. The architect coordinates but cannot alter structural member sizes without structural engineer approval.
codeRef: AIA A201 Â§1.2.1; standard drawing notes
examTip: The architect coordinates but structural engineer owns structural design decisions â€” a common conflict arises at beam/column interfaces with architectural finishes or ceiling heights.

back: Standard form for the agreement between the general contractor and each subcontractor. Flows down the prime contract terms, incorporates A201 General Conditions, and defines subcontract scope, schedule, and payment. The GC cannot require payment terms stricter than those in the prime contract.
examTip: A401 flows down AIA A201 â€” subcontractor obligations track GC obligations. The exam tests the relationship between prime and sub contract terms and who is responsible for what.

back: Before bidding, prospective bidders submit questions (RFIs) to clarify the bid documents. The architect responds through an Addendum issued to all bidders simultaneously. A bid document deadline for pre-bid RFIs (typically 7â€“10 days before bid date) ensures responses can be incorporated. Late RFIs may not receive responses.
codeRef: AIA A701 Instructions to Bidders Â§3; AIA A701 Â§5
examTip: All pre-bid questions must be answered by Addendum to all bidders â€” verbal answers to one bidder are improper and may create bid protest grounds.

front: Substitution Request â€” During Bidding vs. During Construction
back: During bidding: prospective bidders submit substitution requests before bid date; architect evaluates and, if approved, issues an Addendum. During construction: contractor submits after award; architect reviews and approves or rejects per Section 01 25 00. Substitutions after award may require contract modification if they affect cost or schedule.
examTip: Substitution during bidding = Addendum; during construction = written request with full technical data. The architect's approval does not relieve the contractor of responsibility for performance.

front: Warranty Period â€” 1-Year Standard vs. Extended
back: AIA A201 Â§12.2.2 requires the contractor to correct defective work for 1 year from Substantial Completion. Manufacturers may offer longer warranties (e.g., 20-yr roofing, 10-yr windows). The spec should require the contractor to pass through manufacturer warranties. Extended warranties require explicit specification language.
codeRef: AIA A201 Â§12.2; warranty specification sections
examTip: The 1-year correction period is a contractual obligation â€” not a limit on owner's legal remedies. Owner may still have statutory or implied warranty rights beyond 1 year.

back: The owner provides the geotechnical investigation report (boring logs, soil classification, bearing capacity, recommendations) to the design team as owner-furnished information per AIA B101 Â§5.4. The structural engineer uses it for foundation design. If the owner fails to provide it, the architect can request it or identify it as a gap in information.
codeRef: AIA B101-2017 Â§5.4; ASTM D1586 (Standard Penetration Test)
examTip: Geotechnical report is owner-furnished â€” the architect uses it but is not responsible for its accuracy. If site conditions differ from the report, it may trigger a differing site conditions claim.

front: BIM Execution Plan (BEP) â€” Purpose and Content
back: A project-specific document establishing how Building Information Modeling will be used: software platforms, model ownership, LOD milestones, file-sharing protocols, clash detection process, and model handoff at project completion. Required for projects using AIA E203 (BIM protocol exhibit). Agreed by all parties before design begins.
codeRef: AIA E203-2013; Penn State BIM Execution Planning Guide
examTip: The BEP prevents BIM coordination failures by establishing rules upfront â€” who models what, at what detail level, and how conflicts are resolved.

front: Keyed Detail vs. Wall Section â€” Drawing Coordination
back: Wall section: a full-height vertical cut showing all construction layers from foundation to roof or floor-to-floor. Keyed detail: a larger-scale blow-up of a specific junction or condition (window sill, parapet, base of wall) referenced by a key bubble on the plan or section. Keyed details must match and be consistent with the wall section they enlarge.
codeRef: AIA CAD Layer Guidelines; NCS National CAD Standard
examTip: Inconsistencies between wall sections and keyed details are a common CD coordination error â€” the larger scale detail governs the specific condition it depicts.

back: The door schedule tabulates all doors by mark number and includes: door size (width Ã— height), material, thickness, frame type, hardware set, fire rating, undercut, and any special requirements (vision panel, louver). Coordinated with door plans and hardware specifications (Division 08). Must match the floor plan and elevation drawings.
examTip: Door schedules are the primary coordination document between architectural, structural (openings), and hardware â€” inconsistencies between the schedule and plans are a common RFI source.

front: Fire-Rated Assembly â€” UL Listing vs. IBC Prescriptive
back: Two paths to demonstrate compliance for fire-rated wall and floor-ceiling assemblies: UL Directory: a tested assembly with a specific UL design number â€” must be built exactly as listed. IBC Prescriptive: Chapter 7 tables allow certain assemblies based on material type and thickness without a tested listing. UL-listed assemblies offer more certainty; IBC prescriptive allows more flexibility.
codeRef: IBC Chapter 7; UL Fire Resistance Directory
examTip: If a UL-listed assembly is specified, the contractor must follow the listing exactly â€” substituting materials voids the listing. This is a common field inspection issue.

front: AIA A201 Â§3.12 â€” Contractor's Submittal Obligations
back: The contractor must review, approve, and stamp submittals before sending to the architect. The contractor's stamp certifies that materials conform to contract documents. The architect reviews for design intent only â€” not for means and methods or dimensions the contractor must verify. Contractor retains responsibility for field measurements and construction procedures.
examTip: The architect's submittal review does NOT relieve the contractor of responsibility for errors or omissions in the submittal. The contractor must verify dimensions and field conditions.

front: AIA A201 Â§9.4 â€” Architect's Review of Payment Application
back: After receiving a completed payment application and G702/G703, the architect has 7 days to review and either issue a Certificate for Payment or notify the contractor of reasons for withholding. The architect certifies the amount they determine is properly due â€” not necessarily the amount requested.
examTip: 7 days is the architect's review window. Failure to act within 7 days does not automatically approve the application â€” but prolonged inaction may create an owner-caused delay.

front: Owner's Right to Stop Work (AIA A201 Â§2.3)
back: The owner may order the contractor to stop work if the contractor fails to carry out work in accordance with the contract documents. This right does not require cause related to safety â€” it is a general right to halt work for breach. Stopping work does not constitute termination; the owner may restart after the breach is corrected.
examTip: Owner stop work order is a remedy for nonconforming work â€” distinct from suspension (Â§14.3) and termination (Â§14.2). The exam tests which section applies to each situation.

front: Contractor's Right to Stop Work (AIA A201 Â§9.7)
back: If the architect fails to issue a Certificate for Payment or the owner fails to make payment within the required period, the contractor may â€” after 7 additional days' written notice â€” stop work until payment is received. The contractor is entitled to an appropriate adjustment in contract sum (for additional costs) and contract time.
examTip: Contractor can stop work only after proper notice â€” cannot walk off the job unilaterally. The 7-day notice requirement is tested in payment-dispute scenarios.

back: IBC Chapter 17 requires special inspections (SI) for work that requires expertise beyond typical building inspections: concrete, masonry, steel connections, high-strength bolting, welding, soils, pile driving, sprayed fire-resistant materials, smoke-control systems. Owner hires the Special Inspector; the architect coordinates and includes SI requirements in the structural drawings.
examTip: Special inspectors are hired by the owner (not the contractor) to maintain independence. The Statement of Special Inspections (TR1 in NYC) is prepared by the structural engineer.

front: Concrete Water/Cement Ratio â€” Effect on Strength and Durability
back: Lower w/c ratio = higher compressive strength and greater durability (denser paste, less permeability). Higher w/c = easier workability but weaker, more porous concrete. ACI 318 sets maximum w/c ratios for exposure conditions: 0.40 for severe exposure, 0.45 for moderate, 0.50 for normal. Admixtures (plasticizers) allow low w/c with adequate workability.
examTip: For every 0.1 increase in w/c ratio, compressive strength decreases approximately 1,500 psi. The exam tests the inverse relationship: lower w/c = stronger, less permeable concrete.

front: Masonry Mortar Types â€” M, S, N, O
back: Type M: highest strength (â‰¥2,500 psi) â€” below-grade, foundations, retaining walls. Type S: high strength (â‰¥1,800 psi) â€” at or below grade, exterior exposed masonry, high lateral load. Type N: medium strength (â‰¥750 psi) â€” general above-grade exterior masonry, most common. Type O: low strength (â‰¥350 psi) â€” interior non-loadbearing partitions only.
examTip: M and S for below-grade and structural; N for general exterior use; O only for interior non-structural. Mortar type is often overlooked on specifications â€” a common exam topic.

front: Weld Types â€” CJP vs. PJP (Structural Steel)
back: CJP (Complete Joint Penetration): weld penetrates full thickness of base metal â€” carries full load in tension, shear, and compression. Requires backing bar and ultrasonic testing (UT). PJP (Partial Joint Penetration): weld does not penetrate full thickness â€” limited load capacity, used where full-strength connection is not required. CJP is required for moment connections in seismic design.
codeRef: AISC 360; AWS D1.1 Structural Welding Code
examTip: CJP = full strength, requires UT inspection; PJP = partial strength, requires MT or PT inspection. The exam tests which weld type is required for seismic moment connections.

front: High-Strength Bolt Installation â€” Snug-Tight vs. Pretensioned vs. Slip-Critical
back: Snug-tight: tightened until full effort of an ironworker's spud wrench â€” minimum installation for bearing-type connections. Pretensioned: bolts tensioned to a minimum tension (70% of tensile strength) using turn-of-nut, calibrated wrench, or DTI washers â€” required for seismic and wind connections. Slip-critical: pretensioned with faying surface requirements â€” must prevent slip at service load.
codeRef: AISC 360 Â§J3; RCSC Specification for Structural Joints
examTip: Seismic connections require at least pretensioned; slip-critical requires both pretension and a prepared (Class A or B) faying surface. Inspection methods differ for each installation method.

front: Commissioning â€” Purpose and Authority Having Jurisdiction
back: Building commissioning (Cx) is a systematic quality assurance process that verifies installed building systems (HVAC, controls, plumbing, fire protection) perform as intended per the owner's project requirements (OPR) and basis of design (BOD). A Commissioning Authority (CxA) leads the process. LEED EA Prerequisite 1 requires fundamental commissioning for all projects seeking LEED certification.
codeRef: ASHRAE Guideline 0-2019; LEED v4 EA Prerequisite
examTip: The CxA is ideally independent of the design team â€” third-party commissioning is required for LEED Enhanced Commissioning. Cx begins in design, not at construction completion.

front: Concrete Slump Test â€” Purpose and Limit
back: ASTM C143: measures workability of fresh concrete by the vertical settlement (slump) of a cone of concrete after the mold is removed. Typical design slump: 3â€“5 inches for structural concrete. Slump > specified indicates excess water (high w/c ratio, reduced strength). Slump test must be performed within 5 minutes of sampling.
examTip: High slump = too wet = lower strength and durability. If field slump exceeds the specified maximum, concrete should be rejected (not water should not be added).

front: Concrete Cylinder Break Test â€” When and Why
back: ASTM C39: compression test of standard 4Ã—8-inch or 6Ã—12-inch concrete cylinders cured under standard conditions. Cylinders are sampled at the same time as the slump test. Standard: 1 set of 4 cylinders per 150 CY or per day's pour. Tested at 7 days (indicator) and 28 days (acceptance). 28-day f'c must meet or exceed specified design strength.
examTip: 7-day break â‰ˆ 70% of 28-day strength. If 28-day breaks fail, cores may be required â€” and the contractor must pay for additional testing of nonconforming concrete.

front: Nonconforming Work â€” Architect's Authority (AIA A201)
back: The architect has authority to reject work that does not conform to the contract documents (AIA A201 Â§3.3.3, Â§4.2.6). Rejected work must be removed and replaced at the contractor's expense. The architect may require special inspection of suspected nonconforming work even after it is covered (Â§13.5.2). The architect's rejection authority is not limited to visible defects.
examTip: The architect can require uncovering of covered work to inspect â€” if it was concealed improperly, contractor pays; if it conformed, owner pays for the uncovering and re-covering.

front: Testing, Adjusting, and Balancing (TAB) â€” HVAC
back: The process of adjusting HVAC systems to deliver specified air and water flow rates to all spaces, ensuring that heating, cooling, and ventilation match the design intent. Performed by an independent TAB contractor before commissioning and occupancy. TAB report is submitted to the architect and engineer for review.
examTip: TAB is a prerequisite to commissioning â€” systems must be balanced before functional performance tests are run. The architect reviews the TAB report as part of CA closeout.

front: Change Order vs. ASI vs. RFI â€” CA Decision Tools
back: RFI: contractor's written request for clarification on the contract documents â€” architect responds in writing. ASI (Architect's Supplemental Instruction): minor changes issued by the architect that do not affect contract sum or time â€” issued unilaterally without a change order. Change Order: formal modification to contract sum and/or time â€” requires signatures of owner, contractor, and architect.
codeRef: AIA A201 Â§7.4 (ASI); Â§7.2 (CO); AIA G714 (ASI form)
examTip: ASIs are for clarifications that cost nothing â€” if an ASI changes cost or time, it must become a change order. Misuse of ASIs to avoid change orders is a common dispute source.

front: Construction Observation vs. Supervision â€” Key Distinction
back: Architect provides Construction Administration (CA): periodic site visits to observe work for general conformance with contract documents â€” NOT continuous supervision. The contractor controls means, methods, sequences, and safety. The architect is not present at all times and does not direct workers. Supervision implies continuous control; observation implies periodic review.
examTip: The exam frequently tests that the architect observes (not supervises) â€” if the contract says 'inspection' it may expose the architect to claims of continuous oversight responsibility.

back: Written documentation of each site visit during construction. Records: date, weather, persons present, work in progress, work completed, materials on site, questions raised and responses given, observations of nonconforming work, and photos. Becomes part of the project record and protects all parties in disputes.
codeRef: AIA B101 Â§3.6.1; AIA G711 Architect's Field Report form
examTip: Contemporaneous field reports are the architect's primary legal defense in construction disputes â€” they document what was observed and what was said. Absent reports = absent evidence.

front: Retainage Release â€” Substantial vs. Final Completion
back: Retainage (typically 5â€“10% of each payment) is withheld from progress payments to ensure contract completion. At Substantial Completion, retainage may be reduced (e.g., from 10% to 5%) or fully released on completed portions. Final payment (releasing all retained funds) requires completion of punch list, submission of record documents, consent of surety (G707), and final lien waivers.
examTip: Retainage protects the owner â€” final release requires the full closeout package (G707, record docs, O&M manuals, lien waivers). A partial retainage reduction at Substantial Completion is common.

back: Pre-occupancy (punch list) walkthrough: formal inspection before or at Substantial Completion to identify work that does not conform â€” results in the punch list. Post-occupancy evaluation (POE): evaluation 1â€“2 years after occupancy to assess performance vs. design intent. Pre-occupancy = contractor's remaining obligation; POE = building performance feedback.
codeRef: AIA A201 Â§9.8 (punch list); AIA B101 Â§4.2.4 (POE as additional service)
examTip: Punch list is contractor's obligation before final payment; POE is a design evaluation tool â€” not required by AIA basic services. Confusing the two is a common exam distractor.

front: Allowance vs. Contingency â€” Contract Sum Adjustments
back: Allowance: a pre-defined dollar amount for a specific undefined item, included in the contract sum. Owner directs how it is spent; change order adjusts the contract sum if actual cost differs from the allowance. Contingency: a general reserve within the contract (owner or contractor side) for unforeseen costs â€” not tied to a specific item. Allowance = specific; Contingency = general reserve.
codeRef: AIA A201 Â§3.8 (allowances); AIA A133 Â§5 (contingency in GMP)
examTip: Owner's contingency in a GMP contract belongs to the owner unless spent â€” unused contingency does not automatically go to the contractor. This is a GMP-specific exam topic.

front: NYC BC Chapter 3 â€” Use and Occupancy Groups
back: NYC BC generally follows IBC occupancy classifications (A, B, E, F, H, I, M, R, S, U) but with NYC-specific sub-groups and thresholds. Key differences: NYC requires Certificate of Occupancy for all buildings; high-rise threshold is 75 ft. (matching IBC); R-2 (multi-family) requires sprinklers above certain heights per NYC BC Â§903.
codeRef: NYC Building Code Chapter 3 (2022 NYC BC)
examTip: Know which NYC occupancy classifications differ from IBC â€” especially R-2 sprinkler requirements and the NYC Assembly occupancy thresholds, which can differ from IBC in occupant load calculations.

front: NYC BC Â§903 â€” Automatic Sprinkler Systems
back: NYC BC requires NFPA 13 sprinklers in all new buildings over 6 stories or 75 ft. in height, all new Group A occupancies with occupant load >300, and all new high-hazard occupancies. Existing buildings undergoing certain alterations may also trigger sprinkler upgrades. NYC Local Law 26/2004 mandated sprinklers in existing pre-1984 high-rise office buildings.
codeRef: NYC BC Â§903 (2022); NYC Local Law 26/2004
examTip: NYC is more stringent than IBC for sprinklers â€” know that Local Law 26 covers existing high-rise offices. The exam tests which building types require full NFPA 13 sprinkler systems.

front: NYC Zoning Resolution Â§12-10 â€” Use Group Definitions
back: NYC ZR organizes uses into 18 Use Groups (UG 1â€“18): UG 1-2 = Residential (single/multi-family), UG 3-4 = Community Facility (schools, hospitals), UG 5-9 = Retail and local services, UG 10-16 = Industrial uses, UG 17-18 = Special uses. A use's UG determines in which zones it is permitted.
examTip: UG 3-4 (Community Facility) uses receive FAR bonuses in residential districts â€” a key planning tool for hospitals, schools, and libraries in NYC. Know that UG determines zoning compatibility.

front: NYC Zoning Resolution â€” Sky Exposure Plane
back: A sloped plane above the street wall beyond which a building's bulk must recede to ensure light and air to the street. The plane angle varies by district. Buildings set back from the street wall can often rise higher without piercing the plane. Tower-on-base typologies comply through set-backs above the base height.
codeRef: NYC Zoning Resolution Article II, III, IV (district regulations)
examTip: Sky exposure plane compliance shapes NYC's wedding-cake setback building typology. The base height, setback distance, and plane angle are all district-specific and must be verified in the ZR.

front: NYC Zoning Resolution â€” Open Space Ratio (OSR)
back: In residential districts, OSR is the required amount of open space (in square feet) per 100 SF of residential floor area. Calculated as: Required Open Space = Residential Floor Area Ã— (OSR/100). Open space must be accessible to residents and meet minimum dimension requirements. Quality residential design in NYC must balance FAR, OSR, and height limits.
examTip: OSR limits density by requiring outdoor space â€” in addition to FAR. A high-OSR district may reach FAR limits before OSR limits or vice versa, requiring careful massing analysis.

front: NYC Zoning â€” Street Wall Regulations (Contextual Districts)
back: In contextual (C1-C4, R6A, R7A, etc.) districts, buildings must maintain a street wall at or near the street line (typically within 0â€“8 ft.) up to a minimum base height (e.g., 40â€“65 ft.). This ensures pedestrian scale and street continuity. Above the base height, the building must set back. Street wall requirements promote the urban 'fabric' rather than tower-on-podium forms.
codeRef: NYC ZR Â§35-00; contextual district regulations
examTip: Contextual districts enforce the traditional NYC street wall â€” in non-contextual districts, set-back regulations are more flexible. The exam may ask about the purpose of street wall requirements.

front: NYC DOB â€” Alt 1 vs. Alt 2 vs. Alt 3 Application Types
back: Alt 1 (Full Alteration): major alteration that changes use, egress, or occupancy â€” requires full plan examination and new or amended CO. Alt 2 (Alteration with Minor Changes): work within existing use; submitted for plan examination but without CO change. Alt 3 (Minor Alteration): simple work not requiring plan examination â€” filed as a self-certification with limited scope.
codeRef: NYC Administrative Code Â§28-104; 1 RCNY Â§101-02
examTip: Alt 1 triggers full compliance review; Alt 2 is for most interior renovations; Alt 3 is for minor work. The project scope determines the Alt type â€” the exam tests which triggers a CO amendment.

front: NYC Certificate of Occupancy â€” New CO vs. Amended CO vs. TCO
back: New CO: issued for new buildings. Amended CO: issued when an existing building's use, occupancy, or egress configuration changes (triggered by Alt 1). TCO (Temporary Certificate of Occupancy): allows occupancy before all work is complete, valid for 90 days and renewable. Building must achieve a final CO within a required timeframe.
codeRef: NYC Administrative Code Â§28-118; 1 RCNY Â§101-07
examTip: TCO is common on large projects â€” portions open while others are still under construction. The exam tests what triggers an amended CO versus a TCO and the limits of TCO renewals.

front: NYC DOB NOW â€” Digital Filing System
back: NYC DOB NOW is the Department of Buildings' online portal for filing permit applications, submitting plans, paying fees, and tracking application status. Most new applications are now required to be filed through DOB NOW. Architects must be registered in DOB NOW to submit filings electronically.
codeRef: NYC DOB NOW: Build; NYC Administrative Code Â§28-104
examTip: DOB NOW replaced the paper-based system â€” familiarity with digital filing workflow is tested in practice management sections of the exam, particularly for NYC-licensed architects.

front: NYC DOB â€” Special Inspector vs. Progress Inspector
back: Special Inspector (SI): a licensed professional hired by the owner to inspect high-risk or technically complex work per IBC Chapter 17 and NYC BC (concrete, structural steel, high-strength bolts, etc.). Progress Inspector: a DOB staff inspector who conducts required milestone inspections (foundation, frame, pre-plumbing, etc.) at key stages before work can proceed.
codeRef: NYC BC Â§1705; NYC Administrative Code Â§28-116
examTip: SI is hired by owner and reports on specific materials/systems; Progress Inspector is a DOB employee conducting stage inspections. Both must sign off before certain work can proceed.

front: NYC DOB TR1 â€” Technical Report (Statement of Special Inspections)
back: TR1 is the NYC-specific Statement of Special Inspections form, prepared by the structural engineer of record. It identifies which work requires special inspection, who will perform it, and the scope of inspection for each category. Filed with the building application before a permit is issued. Equivalent to IBC Â§1705 Statement of Special Inspections.
codeRef: NYC BC Chapter 17; NYC DOB TR1 Form
examTip: TR1 is the special inspection roadmap â€” it is the structural engineer's document, filed by the architect. Failure to file TR1 can delay permit issuance.

front: NYC Local Law 97 (2019) â€” Carbon Emissions Caps
back: LL 97 establishes building-by-building carbon emissions limits (in kgCO2e/SF/year) for buildings >25,000 SF, starting 2024. Limits tighten in 2030 and beyond. Non-compliance results in fines of $268 per excess metric ton of CO2 per year. Most penalties go into the NYC Climate Mobilization Act enforcement fund.
codeRef: NYC Local Law 97 of 2019; NYC Admin Code Â§28-320
examTip: LL 97 is NYC's most significant building energy law â€” it targets operational carbon from existing buildings. The exam tests the threshold (25,000 SF), start date (2024), and penalty mechanism.

front: NYC Local Law 97 â€” Building Emissions Limit Calculation
back: Emissions limit = Building Floor Area (SF) Ã— Occupancy Category Emission Factor (kgCO2e/SF/year). Actual building emissions are calculated from energy use data (electricity, gas, steam) converted to CO2 equivalents using NYC utility-specific emissions factors. Reports submitted annually to NYC DOB.
codeRef: NYC LL97; NYC DCAS Emissions Factor Tables
examTip: The emissions limit varies by occupancy type (residential, office, hotel, etc.) â€” a mixed-use building blends factors. The exam may test the general calculation approach or which occupancies have stricter limits.

front: NYC Local Law 87 (2009) â€” Energy Audits and Retro-Commissioning
back: Requires buildings >50,000 SF to conduct an energy audit (ASHRAE Level II equivalent) and retro-commissioning of base building systems every 10 years, in the calendar year ending in the same digit as the last digit of the building's block number. Reports filed with NYC DOB.
codeRef: NYC LL87 of 2009; NYC Admin Code Â§28-308
examTip: LL 87 is a 10-year cycle â€” the year depends on the block number last digit. Retro-commissioning optimizes existing systems without replacing equipment. Know this as a separate law from LL 97.

back: Requires owners of buildings >25,000 SF (and city-owned buildings >10,000 SF) to annually benchmark energy and water use in EPA ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager. Results submitted to NYC DOB by May 1 each year. Benchmarking data is publicly disclosed, creating market pressure for energy efficiency improvements.
codeRef: NYC LL84 of 2009; NYC Admin Code Â§28-309
examTip: LL 84 = benchmarking (measuring and reporting). LL 87 = auditing and retro-commissioning (diagnosing and improving). LL 97 = emissions caps (compliance required). Know the three-law structure.

front: NYC Local Law 33 (2018) â€” Energy Grade Posting
back: Buildings subject to LL 84 benchmarking must post an Energy Efficiency Grade (A, B, C, or D) on or near the building's main entrance. The grade is based on the ENERGY STAR score. Grade A = score â‰¥85; Grade D = score <55 or failure to benchmark. Posting required by October 1 of each year.
codeRef: NYC LL33 of 2018; NYC Admin Code Â§28-309.12
examTip: LL 33 creates public accountability for building energy performance â€” posted grades allow tenants and buyers to compare buildings. The exam tests that the grade is based on ENERGY STAR score, not LL97 compliance.

front: NYC Local Law 11 (1998) â€” Facade Inspection Safety Program (FISP)
back: Requires owners of buildings >6 stories to have their facades inspected by a Licensed Engineer or Registered Architect every 5 years, in cycles tied to the last digit of the building's address. Report filed with NYC DOB classifying the facade as Safe, Safe with a Repair and Maintenance Program (SWARMP), or Unsafe. Unsafe facades require immediate remediation.
codeRef: NYC LL11 of 1998 (formerly LL10/1980); NYC Admin Code Â§28-302
examTip: FISP replaced the older Local Law 10. Know the 5-year inspection cycle, who performs the inspection (PE/RA), and the three classification levels â€” Unsafe triggers a violation and emergency protective measures.

front: NYC Local Law 196 (2017) â€” Site Safety Training
back: Requires all workers and supervisors on major construction sites (new buildings >10 stories or major alterations with 10+ workers) to complete OSHA 30-hour training (supervisors) or OSHA 10-hour training (workers) and obtain a Site Safety Training (SST) card. Fully phased in by 2020.
codeRef: NYC LL196 of 2017; NYC Admin Code Â§28-408
examTip: LL 196 raised the NYC construction safety training standard beyond federal OSHA requirements. The exam may test the threshold (10+ stories or workers) and the required training hours for each role.

front: NYC Local Law 26 (2004) â€” Sprinklers in Existing High-Rise Office Buildings
back: Mandated that all pre-1984 buildings over 100 ft. in height and used for office occupancy (Group B) be fully sprinklered by July 1, 2019. Over 1,100 NYC buildings were affected. Buildings had a 15-year phased compliance timeline. Work was done via Alt 1 or Alt 2 applications with a separate compliance filing.
codeRef: NYC LL26 of 2004; NYC Admin Code Â§27-954.1
examTip: LL 26 is one of the most significant NYC-specific fire safety laws â€” it covers existing high-rise offices only. Know the pre-1984 construction date and the 100-ft. threshold.

front: NYC Landmarks Preservation Commission â€” Certificate of Appropriateness (COA)
back: Any exterior work on a designated landmark building or a building in a historic district requires a COA from the Landmarks Preservation Commission (LPC) before a DOB permit can be issued. The LPC reviews proposed alterations for consistency with the landmark's character-defining features. Minor work may qualify for a Staff-Level Permit (LPC staff approval) without a full public hearing.
examTip: COA is required before DOB permit â€” DOB will not issue a permit for exterior work on a landmark without the LPC approval. This sequence is frequently tested.

front: NYC LPC â€” Type I vs. Type II Work
back: Type I (Minor Work): work that does not affect significant architectural features and can be approved administratively without a public hearing (e.g., in-kind repair, painting). Type II (Certificate of Appropriateness): work that affects significant features and requires a public hearing before the full LPC (e.g., storefront changes, window replacement, additions). Type II requires more time and preparation.
examTip: Type I = staff-level administrative approval; Type II = full commission hearing. The exam tests which type of work triggers a public hearing at the LPC.

front: NYC Landmarks Preservation Law â€” History
back: The NYC Landmarks Preservation Law was enacted in 1965, creating the Landmarks Preservation Commission (LPC), in response to the demolition of Penn Station (1963). The law allows the City to designate individual landmarks, interior landmarks, scenic landmarks, and historic districts, protecting them from inappropriate alteration or demolition.
codeRef: NYC Admin Code Chapter 3 of Title 25 (LL 44 of 1965)
examTip: Penn Station's demolition was the catalyst â€” know the 1965 date and the four categories of NYC landmark designation. This is a key piece of architectural and urban history for the exam.

front: NYC Seismic Design â€” SDC and Site Class
back: Most of NYC falls in Seismic Design Category B (SDC B), with some areas (especially soft soil zones) in SDC C. NYC is in a moderate seismic hazard zone (ASCE 7 SS â‰ˆ 0.35g). Site Class D (stiff soil) is common in NYC's outer boroughs; Site Class E (soft clay) triggers higher seismic forces. SDC determines which seismic lateral system is required.
codeRef: NYC BC Â§1613; ASCE 7-22 Chapter 11; USGS Seismic Hazard Maps
examTip: NYC is NOT a high-seismic zone â€” SDC B/C means most structures can use ordinary systems. The exam tests that designers must check site class for soft soil amplification even in moderate zones.

front: NYC Wind Design â€” Exposure Category
back: Most of NYC is Exposure Category B (suburban terrain, urban centers with close buildings) for wind design per ASCE 7. Coastal areas (beachfront, waterfront with long fetch) may be Exposure C or D, resulting in higher design wind speeds. NYC's basic wind speed is approximately 110â€“130 mph (ASCE 7-22 maps vary by location within the city).
examTip: Exposure C (open terrain) and D (flat open with water) increase wind pressure significantly â€” coastal NYC projects must check exposure category carefully. The exam tests that exposure category is site-specific.

front: NYC Flood Zone Requirements â€” Post-Sandy Updates
back: After Superstorm Sandy (2012), NYC updated its Flood Zone requirements. Buildings in Flood Zone AE must elevate the lowest floor to or above the Base Flood Elevation (BFE) plus freeboard. Dry floodproofing or wet floodproofing are alternatives for non-residential buildings. NYC's Resiliency Retrofit Program encourages voluntary upgrades for existing buildings.
codeRef: NYC BC Appendix G; FEMA FIRM Maps; NYC Zoning Resolution Â§64
examTip: BFE + freeboard = the required finished floor elevation in Zone AE. Mechanical equipment must be elevated above BFE. This post-Sandy requirement is frequently tested in NYC-specific exam content.

front: NYC BC Â§BC 110 â€” Required Inspections Before CO
back: NYC BC Â§BC 110 lists the required progress inspections that must be completed and signed off before the DOB will issue a Certificate of Occupancy. Key milestone inspections: foundation, frame, fire-stopping, sprinklers, plumbing rough, electrical, energy code, and final. Missing any required inspection sign-off delays the CO.
examTip: Each inspection milestone gates the next phase of construction. Architects must track the inspection sequence and ensure the DOB inspector signs off before work is covered or the CO is applied for.

front: NYC Zoning â€” Inclusionary Housing Program
back: NYC's Mandatory Inclusionary Housing (MIH) program (adopted 2016) requires developers to provide a percentage of affordable units in new residential developments in designated areas. Option 1: 25% of units at 60% AMI. Option 2: 30% at 80% AMI. Option 3: 20% at 40% AMI. In exchange, developers receive a zoning floor area bonus or simply meet the requirement as a condition of approval.
codeRef: NYC ZR Â§23-90; MIH Zoning Text Amendment 2016
examTip: MIH applies in MIH designated areas â€” not citywide. Know the basic options (25% at 60% AMI is the most common) and that units must be permanently affordable.

front: NYC Housing Maintenance Code â€” Heat and Hot Water
back: NYC Housing Maintenance Code (MDL): landlords must provide heat from October 1 â€“ May 31. During the day (6 AMâ€“10 PM), if outdoor temp is below 55Â°F, apartments must be at least 68Â°F. At night (10 PMâ€“6 AM), apartments must be at least 62Â°F. Hot water must be 120Â°F at the tap, 24/7 year-round.
codeRef: NYC Admin Code Â§27-2029; Housing Maintenance Code
examTip: 68Â°F daytime and 62Â°F nighttime are the legally required minimums during heating season. The exam may test landlord obligations under NYC's tenant protection laws.

front: NYC Fire Code â€” Fire Safety Director
back: NYC Fire Code requires a Fire Safety Director (FSD) in all high-rise buildings (>75 ft.) used for office, hotel, or manufacturing occupancy. The FSD is responsible for fire safety planning, staff training, and directing occupants in emergencies. Must hold a Certificate of Fitness from the FDNY. Required by NYC FC Â§403.
codeRef: NYC Fire Code Â§403; 2 RCNY Â§10-03
examTip: FSD is an NYC-specific requirement â€” not in the IBC. The exam tests the building type threshold (>75 ft.) and that the FSD must be FDNY-certified, not just any building employee.

back: NYC Local Law 58 predates the ADA (1990) and established accessibility requirements for NYC buildings. It mandated accessible entrance, toilet rooms, and routes in public buildings. When the ADA was enacted, NYC buildings became subject to both LL 58 and the ADA â€” compliance with the more stringent standard is required.
examTip: LL 58 predates the ADA by 3 years â€” NYC has been a leader in accessibility legislation. When ADA and LL 58 conflict, the more restrictive standard applies. Know this for NYC-specific exam questions.

front: NYC Zoning â€” FAR Calculation for Residential vs. Commercial
back: Residential FAR in NYC applies to dwelling floor area; mechanical and accessory spaces may or may not be counted depending on the district. Commercial FAR covers retail and office floor area. In mixed-use buildings, FAR for each use is calculated separately against its respective maximum. Community facility uses often have a separate (and higher) FAR allowance.
codeRef: NYC ZR Â§12-10 (definitions); district-specific FAR tables
examTip: Community facility FAR (UG 3-4) is often higher than residential FAR in the same zone â€” this drives the prevalence of hospitals and schools exceeding typical residential heights in NYC.

front: NYC DOT â€” Sidewalk Cafe Permit
back: A revocable consent permit issued by NYC DOT allows a restaurant to use a portion of the public sidewalk for outdoor seating (sidewalk cafÃ©). Requires DOT approval, community board review, and coordination with DOB for any permanent enclosures. An unenclosed cafÃ© needs only DOT consent; an enclosed cafÃ© also requires a DOB permit and may trigger zoning review.
codeRef: NYC Admin Code Â§20-226; NYC DOT Sidewalk CafÃ© Rules
examTip: Sidewalk cafes are a two-step process: DOT consent + community board review for unenclosed; DOT + DOB + zoning review for enclosed. The exam tests jurisdictional overlap between DOT and DOB.

front: NYC Local Law 87 vs. Local Law 84 vs. Local Law 97 â€” Summary Comparison
back: LL 84 (2009): Annual energy benchmarking for buildings >25,000 SF â€” measure and report. LL 87 (2009): Energy audit + retro-commissioning every 10 years for buildings >50,000 SF â€” diagnose and improve. LL 97 (2019): Carbon emissions caps starting 2024 for buildings >25,000 SF â€” comply or pay fines. Three complementary laws forming NYC's building energy policy.
examTip: Benchmark â†’ Audit/Retrofit â†’ Cap is the progression: LL84 measures performance, LL87 improves it, LL97 enforces limits. Know the three thresholds (25K, 50K, 25K SF) and the 10-year audit cycle.

front: NYC Zoning â€” Special Purpose Districts Overview
back: NYC has over 30 Special Purpose Districts (SPDs) that overlay standard zoning with tailored regulations. Examples: Special Hudson Yards District (density bonuses for transit infrastructure), Special Midtown District (contextual controls), Special Natural Area District (environmental protection in Staten Island), Special Mixed Use Districts. Each SPD has its own regulations superseding or supplementing base zone rules.
codeRef: NYC ZR Article IX (Special Purpose Districts)
examTip: SPDs are site-specific overlays â€” always check for an SPD overlay on a NYC project before assuming base-zone rules apply. SPDs frequently create exceptions to standard FAR, height, and use rules.

front: NYC Plumbing Code â€” Grease Interceptors
back: Commercial food service establishments (restaurants, cafeterias, kitchens) in NYC must install grease interceptors (traps) on all kitchen drains before connecting to the public sewer. Grease interceptors prevent fats, oils, and grease (FOG) from clogging sewer lines. Sizing is per NYC Plumbing Code Table 1003.3.4 based on fixture count.
codeRef: NYC Plumbing Code Â§1003.3; NYC DEP FOG Rules
examTip: Grease interceptors are required for food service â€” not for general commercial or residential. Failure to include them in a restaurant project is a common plan check deficiency.

front: NYC Construction Safety Plan â€” Demolition Requirements
back: Before commencing demolition in NYC, the owner must file a Construction Safety Plan with the DOB. Major demolition (>4 stories or 5,000 SF) requires a Site Safety Plan prepared by a Licensed Site Safety Manager. The plan includes hazard identification, sequence of demolition, shoring, adjacent structure protection, and pedestrian protection requirements.
codeRef: NYC BC Chapter 33; NYC Admin Code Â§28-408
examTip: NYC requires Site Safety Managers (SSM) on major demolition projects â€” a licensed professional separate from the architect. Know the threshold (>4 stories) and who is responsible for the safety plan.

front: NYC Egress Illumination â€” Emergency Backup Power
back: NYC BC requires emergency lighting (at minimum 1 foot-candle at floor level) in all exit access corridors, stairways, and exit discharge paths. In high-rise buildings, emergency power must be supplied by a generator on-site, capable of operating for at least 2 hours. Battery backup is acceptable for low-rise buildings. Illumination must activate automatically on primary power failure.
codeRef: NYC BC Â§1006; NYC BC Â§2702 (Emergency Power)
examTip: 1 fc at floor level is the minimum emergency illumination level. High-rises must have generator-backed emergency power â€” battery packs alone are insufficient above a certain height. Know the 2-hour runtime requirement.
